Finish with Sit down.

🎵 Song Verse


(Sing to a steady beat)


I don't want none of your weevily wheat,

I don't want none of your barley.

activities.activity_text:
1. Keep the Beat on Laps


Instruction:

Children tap a steady beat on their laps as they sing the song.

Example:

Don’t (tap) want (tap) your (tap) weev-i-ly (tap) wheat (tap)...


👉 Focus: Consistent steady pulse, not perfect timing.


🎨 2. Coloured Beat Bars (Optional Visual Aid)

﻿

Instruction:

Display the lyrics with coloured shapes or dots under each syllable.

  • Choose one colour to represent Beat 1 (e.g. purple).
  • Children tap only when they see purple.

👉 Focus: Visual beat recognition and Beat 1 awareness.


🚶 3. Circle Movement Game (Marching)


Instruction:

Children form small circles (4–6 per group).

  • March clockwise while singing the first two lines.
  • At Take some flour in half an hour, march counterclockwise.
  • Be back at starting spot by the end of To bake a cake for Charlie.

👉 Focus: Beat-based movement and spatial awareness.


🤲 4. Simple Hand Stacking Game

Instruction:

Children sit or kneel in small groups.

  • One child starts by placing their hand in the center.
  • Others take turns stacking hands on each beat:
  • 1… 2… 3… 4…

👉 Focus: Turn-taking, self-control, and beat counting.

activities.description:
Bee, bee, bumblebee,

Stung a man upon his knee.

Stung a pig upon his snout,

I declare that you are out!

This traditional chant uses only the notes Sol and Mi, making it perfect for early pitch recognition and vocal development. It is commonly used to reinforce steady beat and listening skills.


Game:
activities.activity_text:
Children sit or stand in a circle and pass a small object (such as a beanbag or soft toy) from hand to hand while singing the rhyme. The object continues moving steadily to the beat throughout the song. On the final word “out,” the child holding the object leaves the circle or moves to the centre. The game repeats until only one child remains, or it can restart with a new player rotation, depending on the teacher’s chosen structure.

This activity reinforces steady beat, social interaction, and careful listening—all while reinforcing pitch concepts through an engaging and playful routine.

activities.activity_text: Children use rhythm sticks to imitate a train’s movements, sliding doors, and whistles while following a familiar melody, developing coordination, rhythm, and gross motor skills. They also practise listening, tempo changes, and expressive actions, supporting musical awareness and social participation.

activities.activity_text:
Apple Tree is a gem. . . that's the best way I can describe it. It's got a rhythm that is easy for my 1st graders to derive, is great for teaching so-mi (extracted in the first and third phrases), is great for la (extracted in the 2nd) and for do (the final phrase of the song). What's more is it has a fun game that the kids ask for over and over and over and over and over. . . . . and over again for. If you're not familiar with the song, here it is:




The game:

Formation: standing circle, holding hands, with two people (I use myself and a student volunteer) making and arch.


Action:  as the song is sung, the students process in a circular motion with the circle going under the arch formed by the teacher and student volunteer. On the word "out" the arch lowers their arms, catching whoever is under the tree at that time. The caught student then joins the arch (I refer to it as the tree). The game is repeated until all the "apples" (the students in the walking circle) are caught. As the tree grows, more students are caught. The students love this!


As I mentioned, this song is GREAT for 1st and 2nd grades. I use it for ta & ti-ti reading and so-mi-la reading in the first grade. I have created sets of tone ladders that the students use to sing the phrases. After they have derived the known solfa (the first three phrases of the song), they sing it while pointing to the solfége apples on their tone ladder.

We wave our scarves together, because it’f fun to do!

Repeat with more actions: wiggle our scarves together, shake our scarves together, circle our scarves together, toss our scarves together, etc.

Musical Conversation Practice


• Teacher sings a question (e.g. Is my shoe ready?)

• Students improvise musical responses (solo and group)

• Role Play Game: Cobbler & Customer

• One student (customer) presents a toy shoe and gives a tim(e)?

• The shoe is secretly passed around while the class sings

• Customer returns and sings to the cobbler; cobbler replies not ready

• Repeat this interaction 2–3 times for comic effect

activities.activity_text: Children explore pitch using hand signals (Sol and Mi) while singing and calling for the puppet Bailey, developing early musical literacy. They also practice listening, following actions, and expressing emotions through gestures, supporting social, communication, and physical development.

activities.activity_text:
1. How Many Sounds?

  • Go through the children’s names and play the syllables on a drum
  • T starts with their own name as a model
  • Children join in, one at a time

2. Rhythm Recognition

  • PS stand up when T plays their rhythm
  • Listen carefully and match the rhythm to your name!

3. Our Own Percussion Band

  • T explains the meaning of percussion
  • Optional: show a video (BBC?) or eBook about percussion instruments
  • T teaches the children a song
  • Children create shakers using the items they found in Week 3
  • Sing the song and play the beat using their own instruments

Verse 1

Won't my mommy be so proud of me,

Ouch! It stung me!


Actions: Cup hands gently as if carrying a bee, sway side to side. On "Ouch! It stung me!" - shake hand dramatically with pained expression.


Verse 2


I'm squishing up the baby bumblebee,

Won't my mommy be so proud of me,

I'm squishing up the baby bumblebee,

Eww! It's yucky!


Actions: Press palms together as if squishing (remind children it's pretend!). On "Eww! It's yucky!" - make disgusted face, shake hands.


Verse 3


I'm wiping off the baby bumblebee,

Won't my mommy be so proud of me,

I'm wiping off the baby bumblebee,

Now my mommy won't be mad at me!


Actions: Wipe hands on clothes or pretend to wipe on floor. On final line - smile proudly and nod head.

Clapping. playing percussion in groups.
Rhythm, timing. Timing, keeping to the beat, recognising pitch.
Children crawl under while you play soft, slow music, then wake up when the music gets loud and fast.
Play bouncy music while children shake the parachute to make soft balls pop like popcorn. Great for rhythm and beat awareness.

Choose three fruits and then go around the children giving them the name of the fruit that they are going to be, try to keep the groups as equal in numbers as possible. The children stand shaking the parachute and listen for their fruit to be called out. When a fruit is called, the children have to raise the parachute while the children whose fruit has been called run underneath and swap places with someone of the same fruit. Fruit Salad can be called, so all the children swap places and the same time before the parachute falls to the floor. To simplify this game two children’s names could be called for them to swap places.

activities.unit_name: People Who Help Us - Steve Grocott

activities.description:
Circle Chat

  • What is a robot?
  • What do you know about robots?
  • What do they look like?
  • How do they move?
  • What can/can’t they do?


Warm up


  • T models saying the lyrics in different ways/pitches.
  • Children can share their version too.


“I am a robot - I go beep-beep-beep beep-bop”

  • Child chosen to lead. Everyone has to copy their movement – they can only use their head, neck, arms and hands – stay seated.


Robot Dance


  • During ‘This is my robot dance’ section, one or two children become ‘robots’ and dance in the middle of the circle to show off their robot moves!
  • Each time the ‘I am a robot’ section returns, everyone stands still and does the robot moves on the spot, copying the leader.
  • Select new robots to dance in the middle of the circle each time the ‘This is my robot dance’ section returns.


Possible extension


  • Move like a robot activity with piano – slow, quick.
  • https://www.bbc.co.uk/cbeebies/watch/lets-go-club-robot-freeze-game

Port — Run to the left of the playing space

activities.description:
This song is often used in educational settings, particularly in music classes, to teach rhythm and coordination through an interactive game. Participants perform specific actions corresponding to the words:


  • Up: Stand up or raise hands
  • Down: Sit down or lower hands
  • Halfway up: Crouch or position hands midway
activities.activity_text:
Good King Leopold, he had ten thousand men,

He marched them up to the top of the hill,

And he marched them down again.

And when you're up, you're up,

And when you're down, you're down,

And when you're only halfway up,

You're neither up nor down.

﻿Keeping the Beat


Children sit in a circle

Sing "Cobbler, Cobbler" together


Action:

Keep the beat by, tapping shoes with hands or using rhythm sticks


Activity 2: Hunt the Slipper Game

Setup:

One child (the “customer”) sits in the middle of the circle

The rest of the group passes a shoe secretly behind their backs


Chant (Optional to accompany passing):

“Hunt the slipper, hunt the shoe,

Find out who is tricking you!”


Gameplay:

While the group secretly passes the shoe, the customer listens and observes

At any time, the customer calls “Stop!” and guesses who has the shoe


Three guesses max

If correct: that person becomes the new customer.

If incorrect: teacher selects the next customer

    1. Teach children the song – sitting in a circle (ukulele?)
    2. Play song to the PS – and sing along with it. My turn your turn.

All clap to the beat

    4. Back to a circle. Give the children 2 groups of instruments. 
Song If you play before I say…. PS play their instrument to the beat. They then play to the melody.
    5. Can you move and sing around the room and speed up or slow down when I do? 
    6. Take turns to play to the beat of the three verses of the song with the song. All play together on 'Is it true what I said' then when it comes to the answer, play quietly for Well no!' and loudly for Well yes!'
    7. Show LS Lowry Picture. Let's move! This is a very quiet piece of music. Listen to the music while you all going about their business? Talk about where everyone might be going. Now practise jogging round (without shoes, to keep the volume down, and possibly without socks to prevent slipping). Travel over a very tiny distance doing neat little steps, picking your knees up in time with the music. When everyone does it together, do the children make too much noise for you to hear the music? Try with just one child. Can you hear the music? Keep adding more and 1 more children and see how many can do the jogging at the same time without preventing you from hearing the

activities.activity_text: This activity develops Communication & Language through repetitive, cumulative verses and sound effects, while children learn vocabulary related to fireworks. It supports Physical Development as children perform coordinated, varied movements (shooting arms, spinning, clapping, stomping, and pulsing hands) matched to different firework actions, and promotes Personal, Social & Emotional Development through imaginative play and participation in a structured group activity with predictable, engaging sequences.

The main purpose of the game is to feel four beats through movement. The children stand in a circle. They perform the following actions:

Acha, bacha, cucaracha:
Tap knees for the first beat and clap for the next three beats
acha, bacha, boo (rest):

Tap knees for the first beat, clap for the next two beats, and jump round to face outwards during the fourth beat (Repeat for lines three and four, jumping to face inwards)

Game One:

The children stand in a circle, evenly spaced. One child (A) goes into the centre with a ball. The ball will be bounced to the four beats in a line: A bounces the ball (first beat), catches it himself (second beat), passes it to someone else (third beat), who passes it back to A (fourth beat). If there is a rest at the end of the phrase (lines two and four), the chosen child doesn't pass it back but changes places with the child in the centre, becoming the new A.

Acha, bacha, cucaracha: acha, bacha, boo (rest):
Acha, bacha, cucaracha: our go you! (rest):
Bounce, catch, pass, pass
Bounce, catch, pass - change places
Bounce, catch, pass, pass
Bounce, catch, pass - change places

Game Two:

The children stand in a circle evenyl spaced. 

The action in the first two lines la ox in Game One. However, during the final rest (at the end of the fourth line the child now holding the boll runs with it round the outside of the circle, chased by the current child A. If he is caught before he reaches his own space again, he gives the ball back and A returns to the centre. If he is not caught, he keeps the ball and becomes the new A

activities.description: Ti, ta, and too - interactive physical game
activities.activity_text:
🎮 Rhythm Quest – Movement and Music Game


EYFS Learning Links


  • Physical Development: Move with control, balance, and coordination.
  • Expressive Arts and Design: Respond to rhythm and visual cues through movement.
  • Communication and Language: Listen and follow rhythm instructions.

Summary

Children become rhythm heroes, collecting coins and avoiding obstacles in time with a visual rhythm score that guides movement and supports kinesthetic learning. They move to Kodály rhythms – ti-ti, ta, to-o-o-o, and za – shown as symbols and actions on screen.


Rhythm actions:

  • ti-ti – two quick hops
  • ta – one strong stomp
  • to-o-o-o – slow glide
  • za – jump high

During the hoverboard section, children duck low to glide under obstacles while keeping in time with the beat.


Learning Focus

  • Perform Kodály rhythms (ta, ti-ti, to-o-o-o, za) with accurate timing.
  • Follow a visual score to connect sound, symbol, and movement.
  • Build coordination, timing, and rhythmic awareness through active play.

activities.description:
The song is often used to practice rhythm, both with macro beats (larger rhythmic pulses) and micro beats (smaller rhythmic pulses),


It can be used to teach specific notes, like F, A, and C, or even half notes.


The melody is simple and repetitive, making it suitable for young children. 


Game: Walk around while singing the song, then pair up with the nearest person when the song ends “choose me".


Animal Game

The song can be combined with animal movements, where students act like different ocean creatures.

activities.description:
Introduce puppet – greetings and introductions activity

Your students will love this. Put it in a bag before class. Bring out the bag, open it enough to see in and shout into the bag “Hello!”. Then move your ear to the opening to listen – nothing. Go to each student and encourage them to shout “Hello” into the bag – each time nothing happens. Finally, get all the students together to shout “Hello!” at the same time. This time the puppet wakes up and jumps out of the bag! Then model the role play with the puppet:



Teacher: “Hello”, What’s your name?”

Puppet: “How are you?”

Teacher: “Hello”, What’s your name?”

Puppet: “I’m fine, thank you”.

Then move onto the first student and say “Hello“ and encourage him/her to say hello back. Next, the puppet asks each student:

“What’s your name?”

and encourage the response:

“My name is…”

Then, the puppet asks each student:

“How are you?”

And elicit:

“I’m fine, thank you.”

Finally, the puppet says “Goodbye” and “See you” before going back into the bag to sleep.

activities.activity: Shake hands up in - Physical Starters
activities.description: Shake hands up in the air as you chant 'And an up, shake, shake, shake, shakedy shake', 'and a down shake, shake, shake, shakedy shake' with the appropriate actions. Repeat twice before 'To the front, to the back, to the s-s-side' x2. Then 'Go around and around' with a stirring type action, as if stirring something in a large cauldron. Repeat but getting gradually faster.

activities.activity: Rub hands together, shake - Physical Starters
activities.description: Rub hands together, shake hands out and in and up and down. Mix the order up.

activities.activity: Put feet together and - Physical Starters
activities.description: Put feet together and raise arms in a v-shape above the head. Bend over to the right then to the left which will help to stretch singing muscles.

activities.activity: Check posture by making - Physical Starters
activities.description: Check posture by making sure that ears are over shoulders. Shoulders are over hips and hips are over ankles. Stretch both hands up into the air so that the rib cage is opened up. As the arms are lowered, try to keep the same openness in the rib cage.

activities.activity: Sing My bonny lies - Physical Starters
activities.description: Sing 'My bonny lies over the ocean', standing up and sitting down each time a word is sung that begins with the letter B. Then add a clap on every word which contains the letter 'O'.

activities.activity: Imagine you are having - Physical Starters
activities.description: Imagine you are having a bit of a shower – quickly tap with both hands up your legs, along both arms, down your front etc.

activities.activity: Raise one arm over - Physical Starters
activities.description: Raise one arm over your head, one hand on hip, then swap over getting gradually faster.

activities.activity: Turn head to left, - Physical Starters
activities.description: Turn head to left, right, back to the centre and down, as if holding a small apple between your chin and chest, returning head to its central position.

activities.activity: Hold out both hands - Physical Starters
activities.description: Hold out both hands with fingers spread – explain that when I call out '10', this is what they show. When '5' is called out they spread the fingers of one hand out whilst making a fist with the other hand. When 'nothing' is called out they make a fist with both hands. Add 'a different 5' & 'Faster'. Then add 'Wiggle your 10', 'Wave your `10', 'Shake your 10', 'Flick your 10', 'Clasp together and squeeze', 'Turn them inside out', 'Stretch them up to the ceiling', 'Let go'. Add 'Shake arms', 'Bend your knees down'.

activities.activity: Find a good standing - Physical Starters
activities.description: Find a good standing position and lift up one shoulder then two. Take their shoulders swimming, doing the front crawl, back stroke and butterfly strokes!

activities.activity: Pretend you are wearing - Breathing Exercises
activities.description: Pretend you are wearing a great big inflatable rubber ring all the way around your middle. What you're going to do is to push the ring out as you breathe in over the count of 4 without making a single noise. Then breathe out very quietly over a count of 4. Keep the shoulders completely relaxed and down. Your lungs will work without you having to use your shoulders.

activities.activity: Place your hands underneath - Breathing Exercises
activities.description: Place your hands underneath your rib cage, breathe in and out in your own time but try to get the sensation of breathing under where your hands are. Try to breathe deeply. Repeat but with their hands together as in prayer.

activities.activity: Hold out one hand - Breathing Exercises
activities.description: Hold out one hand in front of you, palm facing towards you. Imagine that each finger is a candle. Blow out each candle in turn and then on the last candle blow out any remaining breath. Repeat with the other hand under your belly button. Repeat with the following sounds: ff; ff ff ff ff ; ssh ssh ssh ssh ssh.

activities.activity: Say Ssh, ssh, ssh, - Breathing Exercises
activities.description: Say 'Ssh, ssh, ssh, sshhhhh' in rhythm. Then incorporate it giving yourself a shower. Set the challenge of while the water is running down – you're going to be the water going down using the 'Ssh' sound over a count of 4. You have that count of 4 to wash all over and then return to rubbing your hands in front of you. Then repeat it over a count of 3. However, ask a child to suggest where everyone must wash eg. behind your ears, between your toes etc. Repeat over a count of 2 then account of 1.

activities.activity: Stretch one arm out - Breathing Exercises
activities.description: Stretch one arm out in front of them with one finger pointing up, imagining there is a flame on the end of it. Without bending towards it they are to try and blow it out with short puffs. Can they feel the air on their finger and their diaphragm muscle working?

activities.activity: Make your lips very - Facial Warm-ups/Diction
activities.description: Make your lips very loose and floppy and do a lip-roll to a 'p' sound, like a horse. Repeat to a rolled 'r'.

activities.activity: Loosen the face by - Facial Warm-ups/Diction
activities.description: Loosen the face by imagining they are chewing a great big toffee. Squeeze the eyes together as if squinting from the sun then blow the cheeks out. Then chew a bit more and repeat.

activities.activity: Say the words Diction - Facial Warm-ups/Diction
activities.description: Say the words 'Diction is done with the tip of the tongue and the teeth.'

activities.activity: Chant the scat words - Facial Warm-ups/Diction
activities.description: Chant the scat words – 'Shabba doo dabba doo, dibba doo dabba doo, dibba doo dabba doo, dibba doo day'. Learn a phrase at a time then put two phrases together with a count of 4 after both before repeating them. Then practice the last two phrases separately before putting the whole chant together. Then sing it on 2 notes a 5th apart starting with the higher note. Move onto the other note after the 1st syllable of the next phrase.

activities.activity: Sing the phrase Mama - Facial Warm-ups/Diction
activities.description: Sing the phrase 'Mama made me mash my m'n'm's' on a monotone gradually rising in pitch up a scale.

activities.activity: Sirening is the best - Pitch Exercises
activities.description: Sirening is the best vocal warm-up technique. Say 'Sing' and hold the 'ng' sound. Aiming for no air and not very much volume so that you're not pushing too much. Sing a sustained sound on one note first then travel up and down the vocal range on this sound as quietly as you can. The sound should be entirely produced out of the nose, not the mouth, so the resonance is in the nose. To test this, hold your nose and the sound should stop!

activities.activity: Say the word laughing - Pitch Exercises
activities.description: Say the word 'laughing' and then hold on to the 'ng' whilst following the conductors hand direction moving up and down.

activities.activity: Think of an animal - Pitch Exercises
activities.description: Think of an animal to draw in the air. Sing the word 'sing', holding onto the 'ng' whilst drawing this animal. Pitch to rise and fall with their pencil.

activities.activity: Begin by humming mm - Pitch Exercises
activities.description: Begin by humming 'mm' on any pitch. Drop the jaw slightly and ensure lips are relaxed and floppy, so the whole head 'rings'. Next say the word 'sing' and hold the 'ng' sound with the mouth slightly open. The feeling is like a laser beam coming out of the top of the head. Breathing should be relaxed and the sound full but not forced. Finally, gradually turn the 'ng' into a big, open, steady 'ah'. Now make an inner smile as you say 'ah', raising the cheekbones slightly.

activities.activity: Juggling with your voice - Pitch Exercises
activities.description: Juggling with your voice (like sirening) but to a 'Whoah' sound. Follow the conductors hand for the pitch direction.

In pairs, one child moves slowly while the other mirrors exactly. Swap roles after 1-2 minutes. • Builds concentration and body awareness • Great introduction to mime • Develops non-verbal communication

Leader calls 'Let's [action]!' (e.g. swim with whales). Everyone replies 'Yes, let's!' and mimes the action together. • Encourages group play and imagination • Builds collective enthusiasm • Supports following directions

Play music for dancing. When music stops, children freeze in silly poses like monsters, superheroes, or trees. • Develops self-control and listening • Introduces imaginative freezing • Fun way to practice stillness

Children walk around pretending to be in specific places (desert, jungle, snowy mountain). Shout 'TELEPORT!' and name new location. • Develops spatial awareness • Encourages physical storytelling • Builds quick thinking and adaptation

In pairs or groups, one child is sculptor and gently molds others into shapes or statues. Hold pose then swap roles. • Develops physical awareness and creativity • Teaches turn-taking and cooperation • Foundation for tableau building

Mime putting gum in mouth, chew it bigger and bigger until it pops! Add sound effects and exaggerate facial expressions. • Develops mime and facial expression • Encourages creative movement • Fun warm-up for storytelling

Children take turns making sounds and movements for letters of the alphabet (e.g. 'Aaaah' with waving hands). • Supports phonics and sound recognition • Combines vocal exploration with coordination • Links literacy with drama

Children sit in circle, each assigned a fruit or character. When their name is called, they switch seats acting out their role. 'Fruit Bowl!' = everyone switches. • Builds listening and memory skills • Introduces group role play • Develops spontaneous characterisation

One child mimes an action. Another asks 'What are you doing?' Child responds with different action and mimes that. • Introduces improvisation with language • Develops quick thinking and verb recall • Encourages creative responses

Group gradually builds rainstorm using body sounds: tap fingers for rain, clap for heavier rain, stomp for thunder, then reverse to calm. • Develops ensemble rhythm • Teaches soundscape building • Supports sequencing skills

Children walk around room copying character walks suggested by teacher (robot, old person, baby, lion). Change every 20-30 seconds. • Introduces physical characterisation • Develops movement control • Encourages imagination through movement

One child (detective) turns away while teacher chooses secret 'tagger'. Tagger winks at classmates who dramatically fall down. Detective guesses the tagger. • Develops observation and focus • Encourages dramatic reaction • Builds non-verbal communication

Teacher calls out emotions (happy, scared, angry, excited). Children freeze in full-body 'statue' showing that feeling. • Develops emotional literacy • Teaches body language • Entry point to role play

Stand in circle pretending to pass balls of different sizes, weights and textures. Name the object ('heavy watermelon', 'hot potato') and respond physically. • Develops mime and group interaction • Builds sensory imagination • Foundation for object work

In circle, each child says their name with a unique movement. Group repeats the name and movement back. • Builds confidence and memory • Supports coordination development • Perfect for icebreakers and community building

Place imaginary box in centre. Each child takes turn reaching in, pulling out pretend object and showing (not telling) what it is through mime. • Develops creative thinking and mime • Opens pathway to prop use • Encourages object-based storytelling

One child leads line or circle walk while performing simple actions (clapping, hopping). Others follow. Switch leaders often. • Develops listening and rhythm • Encourages leadership and coordination • Builds group cohesion

Children imagine balancing invisible balloon on different body parts (head, hand, shoulder). Change size and weight of balloon. • Encourages body control and balance • Develops imagination and precision • Supports spatial awareness

Teacher narrates simple journey (through forest) and children use voice and body to create accompanying sounds (birds, crunching leaves, thunder). • Develops listening and environmental storytelling • Builds vocal control • Prepares for soundscape work

Call out simple shapes (triangle, circle). Children form them with bodies or with others. Add fun like 'Triangle Monster!' with monster actions. • Combines geometry with movement • Develops physical awareness • Cross-curricular drama for shape units

Teacher calls weather scenarios ('icy', 'thunderstorm', 'sunny') and children move around space reacting physically and vocally. • Develops physical expression • Encourages sensory exploration • Helps embody different environments

Call matching prompts like 'Find a friend who wears red' or 'Find a friend who likes bananas'. Children pair up quickly. • Develops social skills and observation • Encourages communication • Non-verbal interaction warm-up

Children stand in circle. One makes a sound, next echoes it, sound travels around. Then new sound begins. • Builds listening and vocal exploration • Develops ensemble awareness • Encourages sequencing

Children squat as 'corn kernels'. As pretend heat builds, they gradually 'pop' up and clap like popcorn popping. • Builds energy control and group timing • Develops expressive movement • Fun way to build group rhythm

Children mime chewing bubblegum that stretches out as it grows. Add sound effects and big exaggeration. • Develops mime and facial expression • Encourages playful exaggeration • Classic warm-up for creative expression

Teacher points to object and children must say unrelated word (pointing at chair, saying 'sandwich'). • Loosens verbal thinking • Encourages creative connections • Develops spontaneity and vocabulary flexibility

Children try tongue twisters like 'Red lorry, yellow lorry', exaggerating speed and clarity as whole-group echo. • Supports speech development • Develops articulation and phonics • Adds playful language challenge

Children walk around. On 'Red' freeze, 'Amber' walk slowly, 'Green' walk normally. Then switch colours (green means freeze). • Builds listening and body control • Develops adaptability • Excellent for impulse control

In small groups, one child is 'Goalie'. Others 'fire' words (e.g. 'ocean') and goalie responds quickly with associated word (e.g. 'fish'). • Sharpens language fluency • Develops verbal association • Builds quick thinking

Form 'cloud' shapes with bodies; freeze when music stops. Explore different cloud types (fluffy, stormy, wispy). • Promotes creativity and group mirroring • Develops spatial awareness • Links to weather topics

On call, children walk and sound like animals, pausing and changing on cues. Include farm animals, zoo animals, pets. • Enhances character play • Develops animal knowledge • Builds movement vocabulary
Children act as musical instruments using body sounds. Include conductor cues for loud/soft, fast/slow. • Builds ensemble and creative noise • Develops rhythm awareness • Introduces musical concepts

Walk as if huge (giant), then shrink to tiny. Explore different sizes and weights of movement. • Teaches size awareness and expressive pacing • Develops control and imagination • Links to fairy tale themes

Walk like feeling an emotion in motion ('angry stomp' or 'happy skip'). Explore how emotions affect movement. • Encourages emotive movement • Builds emotional vocabulary • Links feelings to physicality

Taggers freeze others who must hold statue poses until unfrozen. Add themes like 'animal statues' or 'happy statues'. • Builds control and fun engagement • Combines movement with stillness • Develops creative posing

Children sit in circle and mime story episodes. After each segment, they verbally name what happened. • Bridges nonverbal and verbal storytelling • Develops narrative sequence • Builds story comprehension
Children pretend to look into mirror and mimic facial expressions made by teacher or peer. • Builds facial awareness and empathy • Develops observation skills • Introduces reflection concepts
Use hands to create animal shapes; group creates a shadow story together. • Supports storytelling and fine motor coordination • Develops creativity and narrative • Introduces shadow play concepts
Children whisper an emotion around circle; final child acts it out physically. • Combines listening memory and emotional expression • Develops emotional vocabulary • Builds group attention
Mime passing an imaginary balloon that gets bigger/smaller, heavier/lighter. • Builds awareness of force and spatial timing • Develops mime skills • Encourages group coordination
On teacher cue, children jump and freeze mid-air in different poses. • Develops timing and physical control • Builds body awareness • Fun energy activity
Walk like wind, rain, sun, snow etc., using body, face, voice to embody weather. • Builds sensory drama awareness • Links to weather topics • Develops expressive movement
Mime helpful vs mischievous characters on cue. Explore contrasting character types. • Encourages rapid role shifts • Builds character awareness • Develops bright energy
Circle passes invisible 'music box' with sound and movement. Each child adds their own musical element. • Builds group memory and coordination • Develops musical imagination • Encourages creativity
Move to different corners based on called colours; add gestures or sounds for each colour. • Encourages listening and group movement • Links colours to actions • Develops spatial awareness
Children show big toothy smiles, then freeze statue. Explore different facial expressions. • Builds facial motor control and playfulness • Develops expression awareness • Fun warm-up activity
Walk normally, then slowly add slow motion on signal; alternate fast and slow movement. • Teaches pace and control • Develops body awareness • Builds movement quality
Children pass waves and thank-you mimes around circle (bow, curtsey, handshake). • Ends session on pleasant ritual • Builds social awareness • Develops gratitude expression
Each child in turn mimes wave or gesture saying goodbye. Create group farewell ritual. • Creates closure and respect • Builds social ritual awareness • Ends sessions positively

Small groups (3-5 children) sculpt bodies into named object within 10 seconds using only mime. • Builds collaboration and body awareness • Develops creativity and group composition • Foundation for mime and tableau work

Circle counts 1-2-3 in sequence; on '1' stamp, '2' clap, '3' jump. Continue quickly building rhythm. • Develops rhythm and teamwork • Builds coordination and ensemble focus • Excellent warm-up for group work

In pairs, children invent silly meanings for everyday objects (e.g. 'What's a teapot?' → 'It's a singing elephant!'). Share with class. • Develops creativity and oral language • Encourages humor and imagination • Loosens thinking for story improvisation

One child (Grandma) faces away at end. Others sneak forward. When Grandma turns, everyone freezes! If caught moving, return to line. • Builds body control and suspense • Develops spatial awareness • Creates tension and dramatic movement

Small groups freeze into scene from story (e.g. lost in forest). Hold 5-10 seconds, then explain what's happening. • Develops visual storytelling • Builds imagination and posture expression • Great for story comprehension
Teacher says a name; child must respond with a different name. Continue rapidly around circle. • Builds listening and mental agility • Creates fun and humor • Gets children thinking quickly

Choose setting (market, sea). One child starts with sound; each adds layer (seagulls, waves), building soundscape. • Develops sequential listening • Builds sound layering creativity • Preparation for environment drama

Sit in circle. Each child gives one word in turn to build story together. See what narrative unfolds! • Develops listening and sequencing • Builds narrative construction skills • Excellent for language building
Children mime animals (using body and sound) while classmates guess what it is. • Develops characterisation and observation • Builds expressive movement skills • Explores physical traits
One child mimes a character/object as classmates guess. Rotate turns around class. • Develops nonverbal communication • Builds symbolic mime skills • Encourages cultural literacy
Each student adds a simple, repetitive sound and motion to create a complex, evolving machine. • Encourages group synchronisation • Develops rhythm and focus • Builds ensemble co-operation

Children pretend to put on an imaginary cloak that gives them a new power or emotion (e.g. 'happy cloak'). They then move and act accordingly. • Builds imaginative character work • Develops quick emotional shifts • Encourages physical expression of feelings

Students sit in a circle and one person makes a facial expression. The next person copies it and passes it on, without a sound. • Develops observation skills and non-verbal communication • Encourages emotional literacy • Builds focus and visual memory

The teacher tells a simple story, and at key moments, the students freeze to create a tableau (a still picture) of the scene. • Introduces non-verbal scene creation • Builds understanding of narrative moments • Encourages teamwork and physical composition

A 'bus stop' is set up with chairs. Students take turns getting on and off the 'bus', and in pairs or small groups, create a simple, improvised scene while waiting. • Encourages spontaneous role play • Develops dialogue and interaction • Builds confidence in simple improvisation

One person mimes an action. The next person asks "What are you doing?" and the first person responds with an unrelated action, which the second person then mimes. • Sharpens quick thinking and verbal/physical dexterity • Develops creative links between actions • Enhances improvisational skills

One student sits in a chair as a character from a book or their imagination. Other students ask them questions about their character's life and feelings. • Develops characterisation and backstory • Encourages empathy and insight • Builds verbal confidence and speaking in role

The teacher narrates a story with lots of descriptive sounds. Students listen and create the appropriate sound effects with their voices or bodies at the right moments. • Develops listening and auditory memory • Builds a soundscape as a group • Enhances vocal and sound-making skills

Students walk around the room, and the teacher calls out different emotions. The students change their walk, posture, and expression to match the emotion. • Links emotions to physicality • Expands emotional vocabulary • Promotes body language awareness

Using simple puppets (e.g., socks or paper bags), students create and perform a short, simple story for the class. • Develops storytelling and narrative skills • Encourages voice work and character voices • Promotes collaboration and fine motor skills

Students work in small groups. One person acts out a given emotion without speaking, and the others guess what it is. • Strengthens non-verbal communication • Builds emotional literacy and empathy • Promotes observation skills

The teacher gives students a prompt (e.g. 'a heavy box', 'a tiny spider'). Students then mime interacting with that object, showing its weight, size, and texture. • Develops advanced mime skills • Enhances sensory imagination • Fosters focus and precision

In small groups, students use their bodies to form the shapes of different letters of the alphabet, or spell out simple words. • Combines physical movement with literacy • Encourages teamwork and spatial awareness • A fun way to reinforce spelling

Students take on the role of robots. The teacher gives them commands (e.g. 'walk left', 'pick up a box') which they must perform with robot-like movements and sounds. • Develops movement control and coordination • Encourages creative physicalisation • Builds focus and following multi-step directions

A 'conductor' stands in front of the group and points to different sections, who must create the sound and action of a specific instrument (e.g., 'violins' hum a high note, 'drums' stomp feet). • Builds group focus and listening skills • Develops musical imagination and rhythm • Encourages collective performance

The teacher announces that the zoo gates are open! Students must walk and act like various animals trying to escape, changing animals on cue. • Encourages creative movement • Develops animal knowledge and sounds • Builds imaginative play and quick reactions

In pairs, students create short, mirrored scenes where they copy each other's actions in real-time to tell a short story (e.g. getting ready in the morning). • Enhances non-verbal storytelling • Develops observation and synchronisation • Fosters partnership and cooperation

The teacher holds up a picture of a character (e.g. a king, a pirate). The class interviews the character by asking them questions, with the teacher or a student answering in character. • Develops understanding of character voice • Encourages creative thinking and questioning • Builds speaking-in-role skills

Students walk around the room. The teacher calls out two contrasting emotions (e.g. 'happy to sad'). Students must quickly change their walk and expression to show the new feeling. • Builds emotional vocabulary and physical expression • Encourages rapid transitions • Develops physical empathy

A 'conductor' stands in front of the group. The group produces a single, continuous sound. The conductor uses hand gestures to make the sound louder, softer, faster, slower, and cut out sections. • Teaches dynamics and tempo in sound • Develops non-verbal communication • Builds group listening and response

Students walk normally. On cue, they change to a very slow, giant-like walk or a very fast, tiny-dwarf-like scurry. • Develops control of pace and energy • Builds awareness of physical contrast • Encourages imaginative movement

Students pretend to be different plants or creatures in a magical garden. The teacher describes what is happening in the garden (e.g. 'the sun is out', 'a storm is coming'). Students react with movement. • Develops environmental storytelling • Encourages creative physicalisation of nature • Links to science and nature topics

Students sit in a circle. One person creates a simple sound and an accompanying gesture. The next person copies it exactly and passes it on, like 'telephone'. • Builds auditory and visual memory • Develops vocal and physical synchronisation • Encourages focus and attention to detail

The teacher presents simple scenarios (e.g. 'two friends meet at the park', 'a trip to the zoo'). Small groups of students improvise a short scene based on the prompt. • Provides structure for improvisation • Develops dialogue and interaction • Builds confidence in simple role play

Students are assigned a weather type (e.g., wind, sun, rain). The teacher gives a prompt ('a sunny day turns windy') and students must change their movements and sounds to match the weather report. • Combines physical and vocal expression • Encourages quick, responsive changes • Links to geography and science topics

Place a variety of simple props (e.g. a scarf, a hat, a plastic cup) in the centre. Students take turns choosing a prop and using it to tell a short story or become a character. • Develops object work and creative use of props • Encourages individual storytelling • Builds spontaneous character creation

The class acts out a short, well-known fairy tale or story with no dialogue, only music and exaggerated mime. • Enhances non-verbal communication • Builds understanding of physical storytelling • Promotes teamwork and synchronisation

Students move around the room. The teacher shouts 'Freeze!' and students must hold a pose. The teacher then prompts them with 'What could you be?'. Students can then whisper their idea. • Encourages creative posing and thinking • Develops imagination and abstract thought • Builds awareness of body language

In pairs, students are given a setting and two characters. They must improvise a scene that lasts for only three lines of dialogue. • Teaches concise dialogue and scene structure • Encourages a clear beginning and end • Develops quick, purposeful improvisation

One student starts a story with a sentence. The next student adds a sentence, and so on, until the story is complete. • Builds collaborative storytelling skills • Fosters imagination and active listening • Develops narrative sequencing

Students pretend to be statues in a museum. The teacher is the 'curator' who turns the lights on and off. When the lights are on, the statues must be perfectly still. When they are off, they can move and change their pose. • Promotes self-control and stillness • Develops creative posing and composition • Enhances physical discipline

The teacher sets up an imaginary obstacle course. Students must navigate it while embodying a specific emotion (e.g. happily skipping over a river, angrily climbing a ladder). • Links emotional states to physical actions • Develops body control and creative problem solving • Builds emotional literacy through movement

A line of students stands at the front of the class. The teacher names a characteristic (e.g. 'the bravest person', 'the most tired person'). Students must step forward and act out that characteristic. • Develops physical characterisation • Encourages quick thinking and confident performance • Builds understanding of character traits

Students work in small groups to create an imaginary 'magical potion' with their bodies and voices. Each person adds an ingredient with a sound and movement. • Encourages teamwork and creativity • Builds soundscape creation skills • Develops a sense of group ritual

One student stands at the front and mimes being in a specific location (e.g. a swimming pool, a busy road). The rest of the class guesses the location. • Develops spatial awareness and environmental mime • Encourages clear, detailed physical storytelling • Builds observation skills

Students are given an object (e.g. a shoe, a pencil). They then have to talk from the perspective of that object, telling its story or describing its feelings. • Encourages creative perspective-taking • Develops imaginative language and voice work • Promotes empathy for non-human subjects

In pairs, one person is the 'camera' and the other is the 'director'. The director moves the camera around the room and shouts 'Click!' to take a picture of a frozen scene. The camera must 'take' the picture by freezing in place. • Develops spatial awareness and non-verbal communication • Encourages creative scene composition • Builds leadership and following directions

Students close their eyes and listen as a 'leader' walks around the room, making a specific sound. They must follow the sound without bumping into anyone. • Enhances auditory focus and listening skills • Develops trust and spatial awareness • Builds attention to vocal cues

A leader makes three distinct sounds. The group must listen carefully and repeat the sequence of sounds in the correct order. • Builds auditory memory and sequencing skills • Develops vocal mimicry and control • Fosters group concentration

Using a large piece of paper, students draw a map of a fictional place. Then, they use the map as a guide to act out a story, moving around the 'map' as they tell the narrative. • Bridges visual and dramatic storytelling • Encourages creative world-building • Develops spatial reasoning and narrative planning

Write different emotions on pieces of paper and place them in a hat. Students take turns picking an emotion and acting it out for the class to guess. • Reinforces emotional vocabulary • Builds performance confidence • Encourages a variety of expressions

Divide the class into teams. One person from each team mimes an animal and runs to a designated spot. They mime the animal for the next person in their team to guess. • Builds creative physical expression • Encourages teamwork and observation • A fun, energetic activity

Students walk around the room, and the teacher changes the 'mood' of the space by playing different styles of music (e.g., happy, spooky, sad). Students must react with their movement and expression. • Teaches the link between music and emotion • Develops expressive movement • Fosters a sensory understanding of mood

One student is 'it' and closes their eyes. The teacher taps another student to quietly leave the room. The 'it' student then opens their eyes and has to guess who is missing. • Develops observation and memory skills • Builds quiet concentration • Fun group game

Students stand in a circle and pretend to be different pieces of furniture in a living room. The teacher or another student can then enter the 'room' and interact with the 'furniture'. • Encourages creative physicalisation • Develops imagination and spatial awareness • A fun way to practice stillness and focus

In pairs, students improvise a scene. One person starts with a sentence. The other person must respond with a sentence that starts with 'Yes, and...' and adds new information to the scene. • Teaches the foundation of improvisational scene-building • Fosters creative collaboration • Develops listening and quick thinking

One student is selected to sit in the 'hot seat'. They are given a character role (e.g. an astronaut, a grumpy shopkeeper) and must answer questions from the class in character. • Deepens characterisation and backstory • Builds confidence in speaking in role • Develops verbal improvisation and consistency

The class works together to create a full soundscape for a story. Students are assigned different sounds (e.g., wind, car horns, footsteps) and must create them on cue from a 'director'. • Teaches teamwork and timing • Builds complex soundscapes • Develops aural storytelling skills

Students create an 'emotion mask' with their hands over their face. They then make a facial expression. When they lower their hands, they must hold that expression perfectly still for the class to guess. • Refines facial expression control • Builds awareness of subtle emotion cues • A focused activity for expression work

In small groups, students are given a simple plot (e.g. a mystery, a rescue) and must create a short play with a clear beginning, middle, and end, using three distinct scenes. • Teaches playwriting and scene structure • Encourages collaborative storytelling • Develops performance and acting skills

In pairs, one person speaks in a made-up language (gibberish) with a clear emotion or intention. The other person 'translates' what they are saying, creating an improvised scene. • Develops focus on emotion and subtext • Teaches non-verbal communication • Builds creative, spontaneous dialogue

A group of students creates a frozen picture (tableau) of a specific scene (e.g. 'a moment from history', 'a crowded city street'). On a cue, one person in the tableau says their inner thoughts. • Bridges physical and verbal performance • Develops understanding of subtext and motivation • Encourages thoughtful characterisation

The teacher calls out a character type (e.g., 'a sneaky spy', 'a proud queen'). Students must walk around the space embodying that character. The teacher can give more complex prompts (e.g., 'a sneaky spy walking on a creaky floor'). • Refines physical characterisation • Develops awareness of pace, weight, and posture • Encourages detailed movement

One student stands at one end of two lines of students facing each other. As they walk down the 'alley', students on each side whisper advice or thoughts from a character's 'conscience'. • Introduces the concept of inner conflict • Encourages verbal improvisation and empathy • Builds understanding of character motivation

Two or three students improvise a scene. The rest of the class are 'directors' who can shout 'Cut!' and give a direction for the actors to follow (e.g., 'Do that faster!', 'Show us how you feel!'). • Teaches directing and performance feedback • Encourages critical thinking about acting choices • Builds confidence in making and receiving suggestions

Each student adds a simple, repetitive movement and sound to create an increasingly complex 'machine'. The 'director' can then control the machine by speeding it up or slowing it down. • Develops synchronisation and group rhythm • Teaches the concept of ensemble performance • Builds focus and non-verbal communication

Two students are improvising a scene. The teacher shouts 'Freeze!' and tags one of the students. A third student takes their place, repeating the frozen pose, and then continues the scene with a completely new idea. • Promotes quick thinking and adaptability • Teaches scene continuation and change • A fun, fast-paced improvisation game

A series of exercises focusing on breath, articulation, and vocal projection, such as sirens, lip trills, and tongue twisters. • Prepares voice for performance • Develops vocal control and clarity • Builds a sense of physical and vocal readiness

Students are given a story (e.g., a short fable or myth). They break it down into scenes and use simple movement and narration to tell the story to the class. • Encourages narrative analysis and performance • Builds a bridge between literature and drama • Promotes clear storytelling and vocal delivery

The group stands in a circle. One person starts with a physical movement and an accompanying emotion. The next person copies it and adds another movement and emotion, building a sequence. • Develops physical and emotional memory • Encourages expression through movement • Builds group synergy and trust

Students use their bodies and voices to create a complete environment, such as a jungle, a busy city, or an underwater world. The teacher or a student can then 'walk through' the environment. • Fosters creative and collaborative world-building • Enhances spatial awareness and environmental storytelling • Builds a strong sense of ensemble

Students are given a random, everyday object (e.g. a shoe, a bottle, a piece of string). In small groups, they must create a short play where the object is the central character or prop. • Encourages creative use of props • Develops imaginative storytelling and problem-solving • Builds the skill of creating meaning with simple objects

In pairs, students sit back-to-back. The teacher gives them a scene scenario. They must improvise the scene using only their voices, focusing on dialogue, tone, and vocal nuance. • Teaches the importance of voice and tone • Develops listening and verbal communication • A fun exercise to challenge vocal skills

Students work in small groups to improvise a news report about an imaginary event (e.g. 'a giant cat has appeared in the town square'). They must create a reporter, a witness, and an expert. • Builds improvisational skills and character roles • Encourages clear, purposeful speaking • Develops teamwork and quick scene-creation

The teacher reads a dramatic story aloud. Students follow the narration with their bodies, physically embodying the characters and events without speaking. • Teaches non-verbal storytelling and listening • Develops physical expression and body control • Links narrative to movement

Two students improvise a short scene. On a cue, the teacher taps one student on the shoulder. The student then stops and says their character's inner thoughts out loud before rejoining the scene. • Introduces the concept of subtext • Builds insight into character motivation • Encourages deeper, more thoughtful acting

Each student adds a sentence to a developing story. This version has a rule: the new sentence must change the direction of the story in some way (e.g., by adding a new character, a twist, or a problem). • Promotes creative problem-solving and thinking on your feet • Builds advanced narrative construction • Encourages risk-taking in storytelling

The class works together to create a rhythmic piece using only body percussion (claps, snaps, stomps, chest pats). A student conductor directs the piece. • Develops rhythm and timing • Builds group focus and synchronisation • A fun and energetic musical drama activity

Students practice speaking a single sentence (e.g., 'I went to the store') in different ways, varying their pitch, pace, and volume to convey different meanings. • Teaches vocal variety and control • Develops a deeper understanding of tone • Enhances verbal communication skills

In pairs, students are given a simple prompt (e.g. 'meeting a stranger', 'losing something important') and must create a scene that lasts for exactly one minute. • Teaches time management in performance • Encourages concise and purposeful storytelling • Develops confidence in short-form improvisation

The teacher calls out a setting or situation (e.g. 'a family dinner', 'the playground at lunchtime'). The class works together to quickly form a frozen tableau that tells a story about that moment. • Develops visual storytelling and scene composition • Encourages collaborative problem-solving • Builds an understanding of physical relationships

Students are given a set of cards with a character, a setting, and a problem. In small groups, they must create and perform a short play that incorporates all three elements. • Provides a structured framework for improvisation • Encourages creative problem-solving • Builds collaboration and performance skills

Students form two lines facing each other. One student walks down the middle, and the students on each side whisper different emotions or feelings that the person walking should embody. • Introduces complex emotional states • Develops non-verbal communication and empathy • A powerful exercise in responding to cues

A student mimes opening a box and reacting to the invisible object inside. Another student then enters and reacts to the same invisible object. The scene continues with others joining in. • Develops collaborative mime and imagination • Builds a shared sense of reality in a scene • Encourages object-based improvisation

In a circle, students hold an imaginary rope. The 'leader' can change the rope's texture, weight, or temperature, and the other students must react in unison. • Builds group focus and synchronisation • Develops sensory and physical awareness • A warm-up for ensemble work

Students are given a character and a situation, and they must write and perform a short monologue from that character's perspective. • Teaches monologue structure and delivery • Encourages individual character development • Builds writing and performance skills

Students work in groups to create a news show, with a presenter, weather reporter, and on-location correspondents. They must improvise a full broadcast about a given theme. • Develops speaking in role and improvisational skills • Encourages creative thinking and role diversification • Builds teamwork and communication

Students sit in a circle and pass a rhythm around by clapping, snapping, or stomping. The leader can change the rhythm or speed. • Teaches rhythm, timing, and focus • Develops auditory and physical memory • Builds group cohesion and ensemble skills

A group of students mimes a series of actions (e.g. making a sandwich). One person then leaves the room. The group changes one small detail of the mime, and the person who left has to guess what changed. • Sharpens observation and attention to detail • Builds visual memory • A fun, focused activity for group work

The teacher calls out a list of emotions. Students must use only their bodies (no faces) to express that emotion for the class to guess. • Teaches the power of physical expression • Develops body awareness and control • Focuses on non-verbal communication

The class creates a continuous soundscape of a journey (e.g. from the city to the forest). They must listen and adapt their sounds as the scene changes. • Builds complex soundscape skills • Encourages aural storytelling • Develops ensemble work and active listening

In pairs, students improvise an argument using only non-verbal communication. They must use facial expressions, gestures, and body language to convey their feelings. • Teaches the power of non-verbal cues • Develops physical expression and empathy • A fun, challenging exercise in communication

The class works together to create a physical representation of a story from their shared experience (e.g., 'our school trip', 'a favourite memory'). They use tableaux and group movement. • Fosters community and shared narrative • Encourages creative and personal storytelling • A powerful way to build group cohesion

Students are given a character type (e.g. a baker, a rock star) and must improvise a series of short scenes showing a typical day in their life. • Builds structured improvisation and character work • Encourages creative thinking and sequencing • Develops sustained characterisation

The floor is treated as a chessboard. Students take on the role of different chess pieces and must move according to the rules of the game. A 'grandmaster' can call out the moves. • Combines physical movement with strategic thinking • Develops following directions and spatial awareness • A unique, active group game

Students sit in a circle and are given a mundane object (e.g., a stick). They must pass the object around, but each person must use it as if it is a different, new object (e.g., a sword, a microphone). • Encourages creative object work and improvisation • Builds spontaneous imagination • Develops non-verbal storytelling

At the end of a session, students work together to re-enact a short, funny or interesting story from something that happened that day, using simple dialogue and movement. • Encourages observational and reflective drama • Builds a sense of shared experience • Ends the session on a creative note

Students sit in a circle. One person whispers a phrase to the person next to them. This continues around the circle. The last person says the phrase aloud. • Develops listening and memory skills • Highlights changes in communication • A classic, fun communication game

Students are given a blank 'character profile' sheet to fill out. They then act out their character using the details they've created, such as their likes, dislikes, and backstory. • Teaches character development and backstory creation • Encourages individual creativity • Bridges writing and performance

The class is given a theme (e.g., 'a busy marketplace'). Students use their voices and bodies to create all the sounds one might hear in that environment, working together to build a complex sound picture. • Builds collaboration and listening skills • Develops aural world-building • Encourages creative vocal work

Students form groups and are given a historical or literary event. They must create a living diorama (a frozen scene with no sound) of that event, paying close attention to detail and composition. • Links drama to other subjects like history or literature • Develops creative research and scene-building skills • Builds an understanding of visual storytelling

Students walk around the room, focusing on moving together as a group. A leader can change the pace or direction, and the group must respond as one. • Builds a strong sense of ensemble and group awareness • Develops synchronisation and listening skills • A simple yet powerful warm-up

Students are given two contrasting character types (e.g., 'a brave knight' and 'a timid mouse'). They must improvise a short scene where they play both characters, switching roles on a cue. • Develops versatile and quick characterisation • Encourages fast-paced improvisation • Builds confidence in playing different types of roles

Students practice vocal warm-up exercises like buzzing, humming, and mouth stretches to prepare their voices for performance. • Enhances vocal health and control • Develops articulation and projection • Builds a sense of physical preparation

The teacher gives a beginning, middle, and end to a story. Students work in groups to create a short play that fits this structure, filling in the details with their own ideas. • Provides a framework for narrative creation • Builds structured storytelling skills • Encourages collaborative playwriting

Students form a 'car' with their bodies and must move around the room as one unit, making engine sounds and reacting to imaginary 'traffic'. • A fun way to teach teamwork and physical synchronisation • Develops spatial awareness and coordination • Encourages creative, collaborative movement

In pairs, students improvise a simple scene. The teacher calls out an emotion, and both students must immediately switch to that emotion, changing the tone and direction of the scene. • Builds emotional adaptability and quick thinking • Develops awareness of how emotion changes a scene • A fun, challenging improvisation game

Students are given a short script (or a script they have written themselves). They sit in a circle and read the script aloud, focusing on vocal performance, tone, and character. • Introduces script work and reading • Develops vocal performance and clarity • Builds a foundation for scripted scenes

A box is filled with a variety of unusual props. Students take turns reaching in and pulling one out. They must then create a short, one-minute scene that features the prop in a creative and unexpected way. • Encourages creative thinking and problem-solving • Builds imaginative use of objects • Develops spontaneous scene creation

The class walks around the room silently. The teacher claps once to stop, twice to turn and face a partner, and three times to sit down. • A focused exercise for listening and stillness • Develops self-control and non-verbal communication • A great way to begin or end a session

Students work together to create a machine that makes or changes emotions. Each student adds a sound and movement to the machine. • Fosters abstract and creative thinking • Builds a complex sound and movement piece • A powerful ensemble exercise

Students are given a character profile and walk around the room as that character. When they bump into another character, they improvise a short, two-line conversation. • Develops subtle, internal characterisation • Encourages brief, purposeful improvisation • Builds confidence in speaking in role

A student brings in an object from home. The class then works together to create a short, improvised play that tells the story of that object, from its creation to its journey to the classroom. • Links drama to personal experience and objects • Encourages creative and collaborative storytelling • Builds a sense of community

The class stands in a circle. The teacher or a student leads a fun, physical goodbye ritual, such as a group bow, a final cheer, or a wave that gets bigger and bigger. • Provides a positive and structured end to a session • Builds a sense of ritual and community • A fun and energetic way to finish
Ta and Tii (Two squeaks for Ti) (Extend) Add do – sitting down. Re – on knees I wonder if this works

A welcoming song to start the lesson
With multiple lines
And more details

Teacher sings a line, students repeat

2. Increase complexity
3. Add movements
An energetic welcome song to start music time. Encourages singing, learning, and greeting each child by name.
A playful name song where children say, whisper, and sing their names. Builds community and inclusion.
An action-based song with movements like waving arms, feet, and elbows. Gets kids moving while greeting.

activities.activity: Say Hello (From Your Head to Your Toes)
activities.description: Children greet each other using different body parts and a fun boogie break. Promotes movement and engagement.
A lighthearted song where children say hello in different silly ways (fast, slow, high, low, whisper).
A cheerful hello song adapted from Oklahoma. Welcomes children with positivity and joy.
A friendly welcome song that gets children ready to play by counting fingers, toes, and greeting friends.
An interactive call-and-response hello song with actions like stomping feet, dancing, and playing a beat.
A rhythmic hello chant with clapping, greetings by name, and call-and-response participation.
A calm goodbye song using imagery of butterflies and birds resting. Helps end music time peacefully.
A rhythmic goodbye chant that spells out B-Y-E. Includes group singing and farewells to each child.
A gentle goodbye song thanking children for making music together. Ends with clapping and farewells.
A structured goodbye song marking the end of music time and looking forward to next time.
A reflective goodbye song where children say goodbye to each other with thanks for the music.
An upbeat farewell adapted from the pop classic. Children say goodbye to each other with energy.
A fun goodbye song with sign language for 'thank you.' Encourages waving and moving while saying goodbye.
A playful riddle-based goodbye song. Combines humor with closure to music time.

activities.activity: It's Time To Say Goodbye (So Shake It)
activities.description: An active goodbye song encouraging shaking, stomping, and dancing as children say farewell.
A calm, reflective goodbye song focusing on peace and rest after music time.

activities.activity_text:
1. Take a piece of (imaginary!) chewing gum out of your pocket and put it in your mouth.

(Discuss various disgusting flavours.)


2. Chew it with much exaggeration.


3. Explain that you are going to stick it on the person next to you and that they must then

stick it on the person next to them and so on. You must try to think of a different way of

sticking it. (eg nose, shoe, hair etc)


4.Pass the chewing gum around the circle in this way, amongst much ‘uuurrrrggg

activities.description:
Are you ready to move with me

Slow, quick, quiet, really loud. X2


Stomp like a dinosaur

Stretch out like a bird.

Quiet like a mouse.

Or slow like a hippo. X2


d7, d7/f#, g sus, g sus/a

activities.activity: I'm Going to Blow a Bubble
activities.description: An interactive action song where children cup their hands around their mouths and pretend to blow a bubble, opening their hands wider as the bubble grows, then clapping to pop it. Perfect for teaching about growing and stopping.
activities.activity_text:
I'm going to blow a bubble.
Watch me while I blow.
See it's getting bigger
Watch it grow and grow.
Do you think it's big enough?
Maybe I should stop.
For if I give another blow
My bubble may POP!

Actions: Have children cup hands around their mouths, then open them wider as they blow. Then clap their hands together at the end to pop the bubble.

activities.activity: Did You Ever See a Bubble?
activities.description: Sung to the tune of 'Did You Ever See a Lassie?', this song explores how high bubbles can float. Children observe bubbles reaching trees and flying higher than themselves, encouraging observation and comparison skills.
activities.activity_text:
Did you ever see a bubble,
A bubble, a bubble?
Did you ever see a bubble
That floated so high?
It reached the tree.
It's higher than me.
Did you ever see a bubble
That floated so high?

activities.description: Sung to the tune of 'Pop! Goes the Weasel', this song describes children chasing and playing with bubbles around the school yard (or backyard/classroom). Captures the joy and excitement of bubble play.
activities.activity_text:
All around the school yard (or backyard, or school room).
The children chased the bubbles.
The children laughed and had such fun.
Pop! Goes the bubbles.

activities.activity: There are Bubbles in the Air
activities.description: Sung to the tune of 'If You're Happy And You Know It', this song celebrates bubbles appearing everywhere - in the air, in hair, and all around. Repetitive structure makes it easy for young children to learn and join in.
activities.activity_text:
There are bubbles in the air, in the air.
There are bubbles in my hair, in my hair.
There are bubbles in the air,
There are bubbles in my hair.
There are bubbles everywhere, everywhere!

activities.description: Sung to the tune of 'I'm Forever Blowing Bubbles', this classic song describes bubbles flying high, reaching toward the sky, and then falling and fading away. A beautiful, poetic song about the temporary nature of bubbles.
activities.activity_text:
I'm forever blowing bubbles.
Pretty bubbles in the air.
They fly so high.
Nearly reach the sky.
But then they fall
And fade and die.
Bubbles I keep blowing
Blowing here and there.
I'm forever blowing bubbles
Pretty bubbles in the air.

activities.description: A call-and-response rhyme where adults say one line and children respond with the next. The rhyme describes bubbles landing on different body parts and locations. Encourages participation and turn-taking.
activities.activity_text:
Pretty bubbles, pretty bubbles all around.
Pretty bubbles, pretty bubbles on the ground.
Pretty bubbles, pretty bubbles on my nose.
Pretty bubbles, pretty bubbles on my toes.
Pretty bubbles, pretty bubbles in the air.
Pretty bubbles, pretty bubbles in my hair!

Instructions: Repeat the rhyme a few times with your children, then say the first line and have them say the second and so forth.

activities.description: A countdown song that teaches numbers from five down to one. Each verse describes a bubble landing on different objects (ground, tree, house, hand, hair) and popping. Great for number recognition and sequencing.
activities.activity_text:
Five big bubbles floating all around.
Until one popped when it landed on the ground.
Four big bubbles floating high and free.
Until one popped when it landed in a tree.
Three big bubbles floating quite as a mouse.
Until one popped when it landed on the house.
Two big bubbles floating down to land.
Until one popped when it landed in my hand.
One big bubble still floating in the air.
Until it popped when it landed in my hair.

activities.description: A counting song similar to 'Ten Little Indians' that counts from one to ten bubbles, then encourages popping them all. Repetitive and fun, perfect for number learning and active participation.
activities.activity_text:
One little, two little, three little bubbles
Four little, Five little, Six little bubbles
Seven little, Eight little, Nine little bubbles
Ten little bubbles go pop, pop, pop, pop, pop
Pop those, pop those, pop those bubbles
Pop those, pop those, pop those bubbles
Pop those, pop those, pop those bubbles
Ten little bubbles go pop, pop, pop, pop, pop

activities.description: A cheerful song sung to the tune of 'My Bonnie Lies Over the Ocean' about bubbles floating over flowers, trees, and grass. Children sing about watching pretty bubbles popping all around them.
activities.activity_text:
The bubbles flew over the flowers.
The bubbles flew over the trees.
The bubbles flew over the grass.
So many bubbles, I see.
Bubbles, bubbles
As pretty as bubbles can be.
Bubbles, bubbles
Popping all around me.

activities.description:
Bubble, bubbles Everywhere

Bubbles, bubbles in the air

Bubbles, bubbles floating down

Bubble. bubbles hit the ground
activities.activity_text: Start with the body first learning the actions and then try with bubbles.

activities.activity_text: This activity develops Communication & Language as children join in with repetitive verses and make animal sounds and actions. It supports Physical Development through coordinated hand movements with rhythm sticks while singing, and encourages imaginative play with puppets to bring each animal verse to life.

Verse 1 – Elephant


Down in the jungle where nobody goes,

There’s a great grey elephant washing his clothes.

With a rub-a-dub here and a rub-a-dub there,

That’s how an elephant washes his clothes!

He goes stomp, stomp, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes stomp, stomp, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes stomp, stomp, a boogie boogie woogie —

That’s how an elephant washes his clothes!


Verse 2 – Monkey


Down in the jungle where nobody goes,

There’s a cheeky little monkey washing his clothes.

With a rub-a-dub here and a rub-a-dub there,

That’s how a monkey washes his clothes!

He goes ooo ooo, ah ah, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes ooo ooo, ah ah, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes ooo ooo, ah ah, a boogie boogie woogie —

That’s how a monkey washes his clothes!


Verse 3 – Snake


Down in the jungle where nobody goes,

There’s a slithering snake washing his clothes.

With a rub-a-dub here and a rub-a-dub there,

That’s how a slithering snake washes his clothes!

He goes siss, siss, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes siss, siss, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes siss, siss, a boogie boogie woogie —

That’s how a slithering snake washes his clothes!


Verse 4 – Crocodile


Down in the jungle where nobody goes,

There’s a happy snappy crocodile washing his clothes.

With a rub-a-dub here and a rub-a-dub there,

That’s how a crocodile washes his clothes!

He goes snap, snap, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes snap, snap, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes snap, snap, a boogie boogie woogie —

That’s how a crocodile washes his clothes!

activities.activity: Five Little Teddies Jumping On The Bed

Three little teddies jumping on the bed,

One fell off and bumped his head.

Mama called the doctor and the doctor said,


“No more teddies jumping on the bed!”

Two little teddies jumping on the bed,

One fell off and bumped his head.

Mama called the doctor and the doctor said,
