One little teddy jumping on the bed,

He fell off and bumped his head.

Mama called the doctor and the doctor said,

“Put those teddies right to bed!”

🐝 “Bee Beats” – Online Percussion Activity


Summary:


Children watch a video where a bee flies and lands on colour-coded flowers, each showing a rhythm (ti-ti, ta, to-o-o-o, or za for silence). In small groups, they each play different percussion instruments to match the bee’s movements, following the colours and keeping in time with the beat.


What They Are Learning:


  • To play percussion instruments in time with a steady beat.
  • To follow colour-coded visual cues and understand how these represent different rhythms and sounds.
  • To use Kodály rhythm language (ti-ti, ta, to-o-o-o, za) to describe and perform:


  • Quavers (ti-ti)
  • Crotchets (ta)
  • Minims (to-o-o-o)
  • Rests (za)

  • To handle and play percussion instruments correctly, developing fine motor control.
  • To listen, watch, and perform together as part of a group ensemble.

EYFS Area of Learning:


Expressive Arts and Design – Being Imaginative and Expressive

Children explore rhythm and sound through movement and music, performing simple patterns on percussion instruments, and responding to colour and visual cues while beginning to describe rhythms using Kodály language.
Ti, ta, and too - interactive physical game
activities.activity_text:
🎮 Rhythm Quest – Movement and Music Game


EYFS Learning Links


  • Physical Development: Move with control, balance, and coordination.
  • Expressive Arts and Design: Respond to rhythm and visual cues through movement.
  • Communication and Language: Listen and follow rhythm instructions.

Summary

Children become rhythm heroes, collecting coins and avoiding obstacles in time with a visual rhythm score that guides movement and supports kinesthetic learning. They move to Kodály rhythms – ti-ti, ta, to-o-o-o, and za – shown as symbols and actions on screen.


Rhythm actions:

  • ti-ti – two quick hops
  • ta – one strong stomp
  • to-o-o-o – slow glide
  • za – jump high

During the hoverboard section, children duck low to glide under obstacles while keeping in time with the beat.


Learning Focus

  • Perform Kodály rhythms (ta, ti-ti, to-o-o-o, za) with accurate timing.
  • Follow a visual score to connect sound, symbol, and movement.
  • Build coordination, timing, and rhythmic awareness through active play.

activities.description: This song supports EYFS by developing physical skills through rhythmic hopping movements and animal observation. It also builds language and communication through repetitive, predictable lyrics that encourage participation and vocabulary development.
activities.activity_text:
See the little bunnies sleeping 'til it's nearly noon

Shall we wake them with a merry tune?

They're so still, are they ill?

No! Wake up, bunnies!


Hop little bunnies, hop, hop, hop

Hop little bunnies, hop, hop, hop

Hop little bunnies, hop, hop, hop

Hop little bunnies, hop and stop!

activities.description:
This is what to do

Imagine there’s a bird-plop

Heading straight for you!

You do a fancy side-step

It misses by a mile

How can you be grumpy

When your face is full of smile!

Teaching & Performance Ideas


  • Key: D minor (or A minor for higher voices).
  • Range: D4–D5 — perfect for middle-range KS2 singers.
  • Texture options:
  • Sing in unison first, then as a 2-part round (start 2nd group at “Where the stars shine bright”).
  • Add a soft drum heartbeat pulse or xylophone drone (D–A).
  • Cross-curricular links:
  • Art: Students can create paper lanterns to display while singing.
  • Drama: Use gentle movement or tableau to show the journey to the tree.
  • Literacy: Discuss what a “lantern tree” might symbolise — hope, friendship, light in darkness.
activities.activity_text:
🎵 The Lantern Tree

(To the tune of “The Hanging Tree” – adapted for KS2)


Verse 1

Are you, are you,

Coming to the lantern tree?

Where the stars shine bright,

And the night feels free.

Come with me now,

There’s a light to see,

Glowing warm and kind,

At the lantern tree.


Verse 2

Are you, are you,

Walking through the silver dew?

Where the fireflies dance,

And the world feels new.

Come with me now,

There’s a path for you,

Softly shining gold,

Through the silver dew.


Verse 3

Are you, are you,

Listening to the rustling breeze?

Hear the owls call out,

Through the whispering trees.

Come with me now,

Find the dream you please,

Rest beneath the stars,

And the rustling trees.


Verse 4

Are you, are you,

Coming to the lantern tree?

Where our voices blend,

In sweet harmony.

Come with me now,

Sing along with me,

We’ll keep love alight,

At the lantern tree.

activities.activity: 1,2,3,4,5 Once I Caught A Fish Alive
activities.description: Start with the scarf - shaking them to the music and then create a fish shape.
activities.activity_text:
Verse 1

[C]One, two, three, four, five

Once I caught a [G]fish alive

Six, seven, eight, nine, ten

[G]Then I let it [C]go again.

Verse 2

[C]Why did you let it go?

Because it bit my [G]finger so

Which finger did it bite?

This little finger [C]on my right

activities.description:
We listen to “Engine Engine Number 9” and practise keeping a steady beat using rhythm sticks. We then sing the song together and finish by using bells to play along with a video.


EYFS Areas Covered (Simple)

Communication & Language

  • Listening to the song and following simple instructions.

Physical Development

  • Using rhythm sticks and bells with simple control.

Expressive Arts & Design

  • Joining in with singing and exploring sounds on instruments.
activities.activity_text:
1. Listen & Tap
  2. Children listen to Engine Engine Number 9 and tap rhythm sticks to the beat.
  3. Sing Together
  4. Children sing along with the song while continuing to tap the beat.
  5. Play Bells
  6. Children are given bells and try to play along with the video.


Engine, engine number nine,

Going down Chicago line,

If she’s polished, won’t she shine?

Engine, engine number nine,

All aboard!

activities.description:
See Canva link for the lesson.



Introduction:

  • How would you know if someone was a pirate? (Discuss characteristics: beard, eye patch, parrot, hat, flag)

Main Activities:

Activity 1: Pirate Movement Game

  • Walk around room performing actions: steer, climb rigging, scrub decks, land ahoy, man lifeboats
  • Ring bell to signal weather changes (stormy, rainy, snowy, thundery) - children show the weather through movement

Activity 2: Rhythm & Percussion

  • Tap sticks on "Yo Ho Ho Ho"
  • Ring bells on word "Pirate"
  • Respond to three-note musical cue by freezing

Activity 3: Forbidden Rhythm Patterns

  • Introduce rhythm notation with rests
  • Play progressively complex patterns (slides 13-18)
  • Stop when hearing the Forbidden Rhythm sound

Plenary:

  • Reflect on favourite pirate action and rhythm pattern

Differentiation: Visual supports, repetitive structure, child-led exploration
activities.activity_text:
Age Group: EYFS (Reception)

Duration: 20-30 minutes

Objectives:

  • Respond to musical sounds and rhythms
  • Follow instructions linked to music cues
  • Develop gross motor control through movement
  • Explore rhythm patterns and notation

Resources:

  • Bells
  • Sticks/drumsticks
  • Pirate scarves or props
  • Musical notation cards (Forbidden Rhythm patterns)
  • Audio cue for "Yo Ho Ho" and stop signal

After the hello song, explain that there's something different about Bailey. (Eye patch) Introduce Percy the Parrot - T explains how to call him.


Share Canva slides and run through games and songs.

Ding ding dong, ding ding dong.

activities.description: Shake hands up in the air as you chant ‘And an up, shake, shake, shake, shakedy shake’, ‘and a down shake, shake, shake, shakedy shake’ with the appropriate actions. Repeat twice before ‘To the front, to the back, to the s-s-side’ x2. Then ‘Go around and around’ with a stirring type action, as if stirring something in a large cauldron. Repeat but getting gradually faster.
Rub hands together, shake hands out and in and up and down. Mix the order up.
Put feet together and raise arms in a v-shape above the head. Bend over to the right then to the left which will help to stretch singing muscles.

Check posture by making sure that ears are over shoulders. Shoulders are over hips and hips are over ankles. Stretch both hands up into the air so that the rib cage is opened up. As the arms are lowered, try to keep the same openness in the rib cage.
Sing ‘My bonny lies over the ocean’, standing up and sitting down each time a word is sung that begins with the letter B. Then add a clap on every word which contains the letter ‘O’.
Imagine you are having a bit of a shower – quickly tap with both hands up your legs, along both arms, down your front etc.
Raise one arm over your head, one hand on hip, then swap over getting gradually faster.
Turn head to left, right, back to the centre and down, as if holding a small apple between your chin and chest, returning head to its central position.

Hold out both hands with fingers spread – explain that when I call out ‘10’, this is what they show. When ‘5’ is called out they spread the fingers of one hand out whilst making a fist with the other hand. When ‘nothing’ is called out they make a fist with both hands. Add ‘a different 5’ & ‘Faster’. Then add ‘Wiggle your 10’, ‘Wave your `10’, ‘Shake your 10’, ‘Flick your 10’, ‘Clasp together and squeeze’, ‘Turn them inside out’, ‘Stretch them up to the ceiling’, ‘Let go’. Add ‘Shake arms’, ‘Bend your knees down’.
Find a good standing position and lift up one shoulder then two. Take their shoulders swimming, doing the front crawl, back stroke and butterfly strokes!

Stretch up high to the ceiling and drop hands at the wrists, then drop at the elbows then the shoulders, let the head flop then drop at the hips. Then spring into life. Check their body posture is correct. Put thumbs under the chin with hands behind the ears and lift the heads.
Raise the arms quickly and then let them drop and shake out either to the right, left, in front, behind, up high, down low. Have one arm doing one thing and the other something different!
Play ‘Fruit Face’ where they make their faces as big as possible when ‘Big pumpkin face’ is called out and then they screw up their face, making it as small as possible on ‘Tiny little raison face’.
Shake out fingers, hands, wrists, elbows, shoulders, middles, hips, ankles, feet.

Put their hands in front of their face. On the command ‘Show me’ call out an emotion or feeling which they then have to show with their faces – hands apart eg. surprised, happy, worried, excited, angry, proud, sad, delighted.

Tap their head 4 times then shake hands out 4 times, tap shoulders 4 times, shake out for 4, tap elbows 4 times, shake out for 4, tap hips 4 times, shake out, tap knees 4 times, shake out, tap toes 4 times, shake out. Repeat the sequence but halve it.
Bring the shoulders up to the ears as you breathe in and give a big ‘Aaah’ as the shoulders are released.
Say ‘banana’ with an ‘a’ sound and with lips in a smile. Say ‘prune’, pushing your lips forward.

Have a chew, really noisy, one of those chews you get told off for. Then make it really small in the front of your mouth, like a little rabbit on a carrot. Then massage the face, keep it floppy, getting rid of any tension.
Squash your face together with your hands and go ‘Brrrrr’. Then add some sound to the ‘Brrr’ by sliding up and down in pitch. Don’t forget the eyebrows as the pitch rises.
Smile like a Halloween smile on a pumpkin and then make your face as small as possible.

Stretch up as high as possible with hands, fingers to the ceiling. Drop as low as possible and touch the floor, slowly uncurl to upright and stretch up high as possible. Shake out hands and repeat. Stretch out arms in front as far as they can then above their head, open them up and return to by their sides. Hands by the sides, move the right arm down their right side as far as possible and return upright. Repeat with the left arm. Move head slowly from side to side and drop down to the neck.
Lift their arms above the head which raises the rib cage. As they bring their arms down think about keeping their rib-cage up high.

DIY acupressure: Make your hands into fists, like little hammers, and stimulate the skin by gently tapping all over your body. Start with the arms, shoulders and hands, then move to the chest and tummy, legs.
Imagine a pencil on the top of your head and you are drawing a dinner plate with it. How big can you make it before losing your balance?
To a beat, have the RH tap the LK raised and then alternate so LH is tapping the RK x2. Then tap the LL with the RH behind the body and then the RL with the LH behind x2.
Make whisk-like movements with their middles (hips), keeping the feet still.

‘Magic hammers’: Each person should imagine they have two ‘magic hammers’, one in each hand. Imagine 4 sets of nails in front of them: one at head height, one at chest height, one at hip height and one at their feet. The aim is to bang in the nails using the magic hammers as fast as possible. Choose the number of times it will take to hammer in the initial set of nails eg. 5 movements. To perform the set, you have to hammer in the nails at each level as fast as possible, counting each movement out loud as you go. This will involve moving from standing to crouching position in each round! Then repeat but with one less nail: 1234 (head), 1234 (chest), 1234 (hip), 1234 (feet). Continue with 3 nails, then 2 then 1. Follow it with a good shake out.

Give yourself a shake. Imagine you have no control over your arms but they are just two things stuck on the side of your body. Get a leg involved as well. Then give everything in the middle a little wiggle.

After 3, make yourself and your body as big as they can be. Eyebrows are really important when singing, you do a lot with your eyebrows, so getting them raised is really important. Then go really small face, small body, very tense and screw your face up. Alternate between the two and then smile with a terrifying smile. All teeth and no sincerity!

Imagine they are taking a horse out for a ride. Have hands up in front of them as if holding the reins. Take your horse for a trot first, then change to a canter, then a gallop. Then when I stop you go ‘Whooah’.
Emphasise the width of the upper chest, draw a line from one shoulder to the other making it as long as possible. Make sure there is no tension in the shoulders or neck.

Walk on the spot to the beat. Call out a number and on the next beat the group stretch their bodies in different ways for the number of beats called, and then continue walking on the spot. Use the backing track of a song they are working on. Practice a few possible stretch moves before they start.

Stretch up to the ceiling with your right arm as high as you can. Go up on tiptoes, trying to reach a chocolate bar on the ceiling. Let your right arm fall back to your side. Repeat with the left arm.

Step to the right and clap once, repeat to the left, then to the right with two claps, left and clap twice, then step to the right and clap 3 times then to the left, finishing with a swoop up and clap high in the air on the words ‘Let’s Sing’.

Stretch out your right hand. Imagine you are a puppet and there is a string from your right hand attached to your right knee. Raise your right hand and bring your right knee up at the same time. Repeat with the left hand and knee. Then alternate them.

Play ‘Whacky numbers’. Punch the air vigorously 8 times with the RH, then 8 times with the LH, then kick the air 8 times with the RF then 8 times with the LF. Count out loud as they perform the actions. Repeat the sequence, but halve the numbers, eg 4 times with each hand and foot, then twice, then once. Finish the whole sequence by jumping in the air, arms held high in a V-shape and shout ‘Let’s Sing!’ Repeat the cycle again, but this time perform it with a whispering voice. The final ‘Let’s Sing!’ should also be whispered. Repeat the cycle for a final time, but this time use a thinking voice, with the final ‘Let’s Sing!’ mimed as well.
Tap the head 8 times, then shoulders, hips, knees & toes. Repeat 4 times, then twice then once.
Rotate the neck round slowly. Lift each shoulder up in turn under their ears then both together and turn around. Shake out hands.

Begin with the head facing straight ahead. Without straining, turn it to the left, hold, bring back to the middle, repeat this on the right and upwards and downwards. Try again with the eyes closed. Be aware of your head and establish where your ‘middle’ is by feeling rather than looking. Check that chin is not too far up or down. Visualize your head as very light, floating above your shoulders as if supported by string.

‘Rubber Chicken’. Imagine you have a rubber chicken hanging on to your right hand. Try to shake it off counting as you go – 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8. Do the same on your left hand, then right foot, then left foot. Repeat the whole process counting to 4, then 2, then 1. Finally everyone shouts ‘Rubber Chicken’ at the end.
Over a backing track of a song to be sung, eg. gospel style, step and clap to the music.
Practice knee bounces, rising up and down on the feet, springing gently from foot to foot, keeping the knees soft and loose all the time. Add claps in different directions and on different beats.
Then do a slow-motion hoola hoop in both directions, letting the back sink low.
Practice altogether counting in groups of 4. Step a 4 beat cycle, stamping and voicing ‘Huh!’ on the 1st beat. Each time they say ‘Huh!’ they are to bring their arms forward with outstretched arms.

Then do the same thing but with a 3 beat cycle and with the sound ‘Ha!’ Encourage both of these sounds to come from the tummy. This group, however, are to throw their arms towards the ceiling with outstretched hands. Then allocate one line to the 4 beat pattern and the other to the 3 beat pattern and put both together. Count in – ‘1 2 3 4, 1 2 3 go!’ Once practised, the groups can switch over and then move forward towards each other, keeping in line, ending up on the opposite sides of the space.

Warm up shoulders, individually then both together. Aim to get their ‘east west’ line as wide as possible. Stand on their toes then as they come down aim to keep the same height. Wiggle fingers, shake out hands, put first finger & thumb together, then 2nd finger & thumb together and so on.
Shake hands out up high 8 times then down low 8 times, then 7 times etc. until 1 is reached.

Step to the right and clap then to the left and clap. Group to then copy vocal sounds in the form of call & response to the rhythm dotted crotchet, dotted crotchet rest: Doom doom; Zoom zoom; Pah pah; Doo bop; Sss sss; Kah kah; Zzz zzz; Tt tt; Vv vv etc.
Imagine you are covered from head to toe with rain-drops. Shake them all off.
Do gentle shoulder rolls: try micro-circles and then bigger circles, both forward and back, first with each shoulder and then both together.

activities.activity: Rub Hands Together Wiggle Fingers And Shake
activities.description: Rub hands together, wiggle fingers and shake hands high and low.
Imagine you have a pencil on the end of your nose and a sheet of paper in front of you. Slowly draw a lazy figure of 8 (on its side) with your imaginary pencil.
Head turns: Slowly, while taking deep breaths, turn the head to look left and right. Repeat 3 or 4 times.
Head tilts: Again, very slowly and while breathing, tilt the head to stretch each side of the neck. Never stretch too far – stop before it becomes uncomfortable. Repeat 3 or 4 times.
Shoulders and upper back: Bring your arms together over your head, hold your hands together and stretch. With your elbows straight, reach behind you and stretch your arms.
Stretch up as if climbing up the side of a pirate ship. Then drop arms down by your sides. Chests should be broad and spines elongated. Heads should be facing forwards and not pointing up or down.

Reach up high with both arms. On tiptoe, stretch up and grab an imaginary bar above your head, just out of reach. Release the arms and tiptoes but stay ‘up’, maintaining that feeling of relaxed tallness with the rib-cage raised. Next, slump forward, so your rib-cage is lowered. Breathe in and feel how little space there is for air. Then stay relaxed but stand up straighter, floating up. Breathe in again and feel how the lifted rib-cage allows space for breath to fall in. Repeat sitting down, without slumping.

With eyes shut, lean forward so the weight is transferred slightly too far onto the balls of the feet, then slightly too far back onto the heels. Do the same, side to side. Find the optimum balanced position where the weight is divided equally between both. Bounce up and down to unlock the knees and feel the weight going into the floor. Imagine the legs as screwed into the floor, growing from it like a tree with roots.

Wiggle the relevant part of the body in turn as you chant. Begin with ‘Oh, I never lock my knees’, in a silly voice to which everyone replies in an identical voice, ‘No, we never lock our knees’. Continue with ankles, elbows, shoulders, fingers, hands.

Shrug your shoulders. Lift them high and hold for around 5 seconds. Then drop them into the relaxed position so they hang naturally. Try two dropped positions – drop and then drop again even further. Arms should be floppy and loose and shoulders should hang naturally in their groove.
Draw one shoulder at a time up to your ears as far as you can. Then draw an imaginary circle with your shoulders. Right first, then left. Then both together.

Play the game ‘Emotional Walking’. Ask everyone to walk around the room, moving in and out of each other. (Make sure they don’t just walk in a circle in the same direction; but find their own path). Call out an emotion and ask everyone to use their body and their manner of walking to show that emotion. Start with ‘happy’ and then move on to ‘sad’, ‘confident’, ‘angry’, ‘scared’. To help them portray the mood, use a story such as move around as though they have found out some bad news, or as if they have seen a ghost, won a prize etc. Allow them to stand still to show the emotion if they wish.

Revise the ‘Emotional Walking’ game but this time vary the intensity of the emotions by using a 1 – 10 scale. 1 being just a small indication of the emotion, 10 being the whole body and movement taken over by the emotion. Call out ‘happy 10’ and then ‘happy 4’ for example.

Miming with your hands, screw your face up like a piece of paper, then pull it out as flat as you can. Over a count of 10, morph from one expression to another. Start with sad and, over 10, gradually transform to radiantly happy.

Plunge their hands into an imaginary bowl of water. Now shake your hands vigorously to get rid of all the water. You have a sudden attack of nerves, and your knees shake uncontrollably. Now you realise you must have ants in your pants, and you shake your bottom wildly to stop them tickling. Can you do all three together?
Hold the thumb and put arms over their head and then bend to the left then the right, stretching.

Carry out the warm-up ‘Funky Chicken’. Shake the left foot 8 times, then the right foot, left arm and right arm and then ‘hoolahoop’ your body 8 times. Do the same 4 times, twice and finally once. At the end flap your wings and shout ‘funky chicken!’

Say the following phrases with the class responding appropriately: pencil (hand facing up by mouth) and say ‘Aah’; ‘toast’ – flat hand by mouth; ‘spaghetti’ – sip in with one finger pointing to their mouth; ‘water melon’ (widest mouth possible as if trying to eat a water melon); ‘Someone’s ringing the door’ (surprise face); ‘wobbly jelly’ (both feet stay firmly planted on the ground with the rest of the body wobbling); ‘fish fingers’ (spread their fingers out stiffly in front of them and freeze); ‘ceiling stretch’.
With both arms held out in front of them, draw small circles getting gradually bigger.
Copy the conductor as you sway to the left then right then freeze. The choir to move with you. Good for focusing their attention.
‘Pomming’ – The conductor points to different people in the choir who when pointed to say ‘Pom’.

Singing is just the same as acting, but the words are set to music. Ask them to use their ‘acting faces’. Choose three focal points in the space facing the ‘audience’ – 1) stage left, 2) centre, 3) stage right. On a clap, everyone looks at the focus point called out, showing a particular emotion eg. angry, surprised, excited, confused, sad, happy, amazed, proud etc.

Pretend you are wearing a great big inflatable rubber ring all the way around your middle. What you’re going to do is to push the ring out as you breathe in over the count of 4 without making a single noise. Then breathe out very quietly over a count of 4. Keep the shoulders completely relaxed and down. Your lungs will work without you having to use your shoulders.

Place your hands underneath your rib cage, breathe in and out in your own time but try to get the sensation of breathing under where your hands are. Try to breathe deeply. Repeat but with their hands together as in prayer.

Hold out one hand in front of you, palm facing towards you. Imagine that each finger is a candle. Blow out each candle in turn and then on the last candle blow out any remaining breath. Repeat with the other hand under your belly button. Repeat with the following sounds: ff; ff ff ff ff ; ssh ssh ssh ssh ssh.

Say ‘Ssh, ssh, ssh, sshhhhh’ in rhythm. Then incorporate it giving yourself a shower. Set the challenge of while the water is running down – you’re going to be the water going down using the ‘Ssh’ sound over a count of 4. You have that count of 4 to wash all over and then return to rubbing your hands in front of you. Then repeat it over a count of 3. However, ask a child to suggest where everyone must wash eg. behind your ears, between your toes etc. Repeat over a count of 2 then account of 1.

Stretch one arm out in front of them with one finger pointing up, imagining there is a flame on the end of it. Without bending towards it they are to try and blow it out with short puffs. Can they feel the air on their finger and their diaphragm muscle working?

Shoulders should never rise when inhaling. Imagine they are breathing into their tummies/belly button ie. Releasing the diaphragm to allow air into the lungs. Breathe in with an open throat, as if in a slight yawn. With hands by your sides, feel you are expanding out at the sides and from the back as well as the front. Breathe in for a count of 4, hold for 4, breathe out for 4 and relax for 4, keeping the throat open throughout. Try to get really full of air and empty. Repeat for the count of 6, 8 etc.

To help the children to visualise how to project their singing voice ask them to imagine a long piece of spaghetti or chewing gum being pulled out of the mouth or a laser beam shining as they sing ‘Sing this note’.
Say the word ‘laughing’ and then hold on to the ‘ing’ whilst pencilling their full name with an imaginary pencil in the air.

Breathe normally. Become aware of the muscles you use as you inhale and exhale. Repeat but breathing deeper, using only the same muscles. Keep shoulders relaxed. Your diaphragm (the dome-shaped muscle that separates the lungs from your abdomen) and the muscles below it are the basis of your air. Find the diaphragm by reaching up with both hands into a stretch and then leaning gently back, while breathing in. Hold, then lean forward slightly and breathe out. Feel the diaphragm move and identify the space where the air falls in.

Check your posture, clasp your hands together in front of you with palms outward. As you breathe in, bring the hands up slowly, switching the direction of the clasp. As you breathe out stretch the arms out. Make sure you are relaxed with no tension anywhere. Close your eyes and do it in your own space.

One function of your support is to create a steady air pressure on which to base a steady, consistent sound. Imagine a candle in front of your mouth and blow it out very gently, using a steady flow of air. Blow it out more firmly, and feel the muscle action lower down in your back, sides and front. Next, try saying, ‘mmmm’ rising and falling in pitch, as if smelling something wonderful. Repeat, but this time gain more resonance and projection by adding pressure as if blowing the candle out. Do the same with ‘ssss’, emphasizing certain moments rhythmically: ‘SSSsssSSSsssSSS’.
Completely exhale, maintaining a good posture, followed by a ‘reflex’ breath in, letting go of tummy muscles.

Completely exhale to an ‘ss’ then ‘ff’ then ‘ssh’ sound. Change this to sounds which are voices eg. ‘zz’, ‘vv’ and ‘zssosh’ as in the ‘s’ of ‘vision’. Add diaphragmatic pulsing, keeping the sound continuous.

Carry out the ‘Steam Train exercise’ say the sound ‘ch ch ch ch ch’ as if representing the sound of a train and the carry out a long ‘sssss’ exhaling completely on the sss. Take a reflex breath in adding a high ‘Woowoo’ for the train whistle at the end.

Explore their breathing. All stand still, close eyes, and wait until they need to breathe. Where does the in-breath start? In their tummy. Placing a hand on their tummy, feel the breath as it rises under the hand on the next in-breath. This is where breathing begins. This is your engine room for singing. Imagine the tummy is a big hot-air balloon and they are filling the air tank from the bottom. Gently blow out air as far as they can. When they feel they cannot get rid of any more air relax the tummy and notice that you will take in air automatically through your nose. Now try the same thing again but this time open your mouth as you relax your tummy. You should feel the air cold at the back of your throat.

Finally, try gently making a very short ‘ha’ on cue. Use no effort, as if a drop of water is hitting your diaphragm and spreading out. Next, try two gentle ones – ‘ha-ha’. Finally, laugh, and feel your diaphragm kicking in.
Stretch their breathing muscles by stretching the right arm into the air, breathe out then sip in several times before blowing out. Repeat with the other arm.

Take a breath in to the steady count of 3: hold this breath for 3 counts then sharply blow out (the number of times of their age) separate candles on a birthday cake (pupils to place their hand on their diaphragm so they can feel it kicking as they blow) then let the rest of the breath out as steadily as possible until it is all gone. The aim of this exercise is to show the pupil what it feels like to try and control their breathing.

Laugh in an exaggerated, theatrical way with their hands placed on their tummies and notice what happens. Be as outrageous as possible with their laughs - think pantomime dame, wicked witch, jolly giant or evil ogre.

Imagine you have a paper aeroplane which you are going throw across the room and it’s going to make this sound – Sssshh. Choose somewhere else in the room to throw it. Now flick it three times before taking aim – Ssh, Ssh, Ssh, Ssshhh. Repeat without the hand action. Ask the group to say what is happening physically inside them.

Explain that the fuel for singing is air. Ask the children to put two fingers into their mouth to feel the shape that their mouth should be in when taking in air. Put their hand around an imaginary glass and take a silent drink of air through an open mouth. They should not tip their heads back or raise their shoulders.
Imagine blowing out a candle in short bursts, eg. fff fff fff.
Exhale on ‘sss’ or ‘zzz’ sounds, like a buzzing bee. Try short separate breaths to ‘sss’ counting 1,2,3,4 and then a long ‘sss’ on 5,6,7,8.
Put a hand on your tummy. When you inhale it will expand slightly. When you exhale it will contract. Exhale on a zzz. Buzz like a bee.
Try putting several sounds together ‘ff’, ‘tt’, ‘ff’, ‘tt’ or ‘ss’, ‘hh’, ‘ff’, ‘tt’. Put their hands on their tummy and feel the muscles kicking to help them make the sounds.
Hold out a finger as an imaginary candle and blow a gentle breath on to it for 8 beats and then 16 beats.
‘Hot Potato’ warm-up from ‘SingUp’.

Blow out all the breath in the body – without the body collapsing – then let the body breathe in deeply, quickly and naturally without shoulders lifting. Bend forward with hands out in front and do the same thing, feeling the breath go into the back on the inhalation. Now repeat, standing up straight; really notice the breath filling the whole of the middle part of the body, front and back (this is where the support for the voice comes from.)

Bend over bending the knees and hang like a rag doll. Note how you are breathing. Slowly come up. Add a sshh sound as you go over – don’t inhale until you are empty – pause before inhaling. Inhale as you come up.

Stress how important it is to breathe filling their lungs right from the bottom to the top with air and not to just use shallow top up breaths. Imagine taking in the breath through their belly button. Imagine they are filling a glass of water up from the bottom of the glass.
Practice bad postures to see how difficult it is to breathe properly eg. 1) Lock knees and stick their bottoms out; 2) Slump their body; 3) Stand stiff as a soldier.
Clap your hands to signal everyone to look surprised and, with an open throat, allow the breath to come in easily.
Imagine drips trickling down your back. Breathe in over the count of 4 then hiss for 4. Repeat, each time extending the hiss. How far can they get to?
Now turn the out-breath into some confident belly laughs: ‘Ha ha! Ho ho! Ha ha ha!’ and feel the sound being propelled from your tummy.

Imagine someone is really annoying you and you go ‘Ssh’ – feel the tummy move. Do 3 in succession at a reasonable tempo. Now make them gentler, but still coming from the tummy. Next use a voiced tone ‘Zzssh’ sliding up and down in pitch.

Explain that all singing requires breath support and when singing higher notes this diaphragm support is particularly important. Place the hands on the lower part of the rib cage, with some of the hand below the bottom rib. Then do a stage laugh – Father Christmas is a good example to mimic.

Revise the importance of a nice open throat when singing. You can get the same feeling by laughing silently. Imagine that you are sitting at the back of the classroom, and your friend has made you laugh but you do not want the teacher to hear.

Run fingers down their jaw imagining they are opening up a book – aim for a nice wide open jaw. Tap all the bones in their face. The idea is to create a big open space inside the mouth – their cathedral, rather than necessarily opening their jaw wider.
Make the sound of panting dogs, monkeys, rockets. Massage the cheek muscles.

Think about dynamic contrast needed in the song to match the mood of the lyrics and music. How loudly or softly you sing depends on the amount and speed of the airflow used to make the sounds. Imagine singing with a lighted candle in front of their mouth. Sing a soft ‘ooh’ sound without blowing out your candle. Now sing a louder ‘Ooh’, this time blowing out your imaginary candle. Ask the children to imagine their voices have a volume control switch like a CD player and use this switch to ‘turn’ their voices up or down in volume.
Sing the following on a monotone: 4321 321 21. Move up a semitone and increase the number sequence by one: 54321 4321 321 21. Continue until they run out of breath.

Imagine the tummy is a big hot-air balloon and they are filling the tank from the bottom. Gently blow out air as far as they can. When they feel they cannot get rid of any more air relax the tummy and notice how you automatically take air in through your nose. Repeat but open your mouth.
Make your lips very loose and floppy and do a lip-roll to a ‘p’ sound, like a horse. Repeat to a rolled ‘r’.
Loosen the face by imagining they are chewing a great big toffee. Squeeze the eyes together as if squinting from the sun then blow the cheeks out. Then chew a bit more and repeat.
Say the words ‘Diction is done with the tip of the tongue and the teeth.’

Chant the scat words – ‘Shabba doo dabba doo, dibba doo dabba doo, dibba doo dabba doo, dibba doo day’. Learn a phrase at a time then put two phrases together with a count of 4 after both before repeating them. Then practice the last two phrases separately before putting the whole chant together. Then sing it on 2 notes a 5th apart starting with the higher note. Move onto the other note after the 1st syllable of the next phrase.
Sing the phrase ‘Mama made me mash my m’n’m’s’ on a monotone gradually rising in pitch up a scale.
Another variation is ‘Mama made me mash my mini M and M’s’ singing the following notes of the scale: 5, 4, 3, 2 x4 as semiquavers and 1 as a crotchet.
A variation of this is ‘Mama made me mash my mini M and M’s on a Monday morning’ on a monotone rising in pitch.
Say the following words: Tibet, Town Honalulu, Lake Titikaka, The Popacatapital. Repeat getting faster and using a range of different voices.
Learn the ‘Robin Hood’ warm-up, singing the words, ‘Robin Hood’, ‘Friar Tuck’, ‘Merry Men’ & ‘Merry Women’ to the tune of the William Tell overture.

March on the spot saying each of the following sounds in time to the beat – ‘Se, ke, pe, te’. Keep the feet marking the same beat but now say each sound twice – double time. Increase this to triple time and finally quadruple time. Switch from one to the other. Gradually speed the beat up.
Sing the sentence ‘Red lorry, yellow lorry, red leather, yellow leather, Blackpool Pleasure Beach, Mohamid Ali’ moving up and down the scale.
Put their hand out in front of them and place on it 4 packets of Haribo sweets. Put a bag into their mouth and chew. Chew as fast as they can then swallow. Then repeat.
Say ‘yayayayayaya’ and ‘mamamamama’, ensuring the jaw is loose. Next make a hummed ‘mm’, keeping lips relaxed so they buzz. Stick out your tongue and lick all around your teeth, top and bottom.
Exercise with the words ‘Chocolate and mini mum’. Sing each of these words going up the scale, chocolate first then mini mum coming down.

Over a 3 beat pulse and singing up an arpeggio – Bb D F Bb’ Bb – sing the words ‘I love lasagne’, then moving up a semitone each time sing ‘Cheese and tomato’, ‘Pizza is gorgeous’, ‘Rome and Verona’, ‘Bella signora!’
Make a ‘Brrr’ sound with their lips and then raise the pitch. Make the sound low down like they are driving a tractor and getting faster.

Make a big, silent yawn, and feel the space created at the back of the throat. Say ‘ah’ through a yawn, sliding from high to low and dropping the tongue slightly at the back. Imagine the space inside their mouth is huge, like a cave or a large hall. Now sing ‘ah’ on any note.
Practice saying the words ‘Zinganingningning’ quickly then sing down a scale.

Explain that the tongue is a large, strong muscle that is very important for both singing and speaking. Like any muscle it needs a good workout to function at its best. The tongue needs to be free and nimble so that words are clear. Stick out the tongue as far as it will go. Can they touch their nose with their tongue? If not, just see how far it will go! Now touch the chin with your tongue. Now, still with your tongue sticking out, can you move it from side to side without moving your jaw? How fast can you go?
Sing the phrase ‘One squidgy, squashy, smelly, sausage roll’ on a monotone, singing one note higher on each repeat. Then sing with a very miserable, fed up face, followed by a smiley, cheerful face.

activities.activity: Chew Blue Glue / Pa Car / Bees
activities.description: Sing the following on a monotone to practice good vowel sounds: ‘Do you chew blue glue?’ (‘oo’); ‘Ah, there’s Pa’s car!’ (‘ah’); ‘We three bees need trees!’ (‘ee’).
Practised speaking the sounds: 1) pss pss f pss pss f 2) tucka tucka tick tick 3) tss---------- tucka ta and divided into 3 groups to put each part together.
Say the word ‘Bogapilla’ in as many different ways as possible for the choir to copy.
Learn the vocal warm-up ‘Toboggan’ singing down an 8 note scale.
Exercise with the words ‘taffa tiffi’. Sing each of these words going both up and down.
Yawn with the mouth closed.
Put their little finger inside the mouth and sing ‘Yellow, blue, yellow, blue, yellow’ down the scale. Make the tongue work really hard. Repeat with the finger out.
Say the words ‘Chicken tikka, mango chutney and a pint of lager’ getting gradually faster.
Sing the ‘Popacatapetal’ going up and down the scale.
Speak the sentence ‘Super douper double bubble gum’ getting gradually faster. Then sing going down the scale. Practise holding the last note on.
Perform the call & response chant ‘Boom chicka Boom’.
Repeat these phrases: ‘Toy boat, toy boat’; ‘Inchworms itching’; ‘Fat frogs flying past fast’.
Imagine they are chewing a magic pea which gradually gets bigger. Starts off the size of a pin head and then increases to a pea, grape, ping pong ball, nectarine, apple, orange, water melon.
Say the sentence ‘The lips, the teeth, the tip of the tongue’.

‘Travel vocal warm-up’. Teach the following parts and then put together:
1. ‘Mind the gap’ (dotted crotchet, quaver, crotchet, rest)
2. ‘Tickets please, umm, thank-you’ ( semiquaver, quaver, crotchet, crotchet, quaver, quaver)
3. ‘Travelling, hurrying’ (2 semiquavers, quaver, rest, 2 semiquavers, quaver, rest)
4. ‘I’m late, I’m late, for a very important date’. (Quaver upbeat, dotted crotchet, quaver, dotted crotchet, 2 semiquavers, 4 semiquavers, crotchet)

‘School Rules vocal warm-up’: Teach the following sentences and then put together with suitable hand gestures for each one:
1. ‘Be quiet!’ (Crotchet, rest, crotchet, rest)
2. ‘I know the answer Miss.’ (Crotchet, 4 quavers, crotchet)
3. ‘What do ya think you’re doing!’ (Quaver, 2 semiquavers, 2 quavers, semiquaver dotted quaver)
4. ‘Gimme my ruler’ (2 semiquavers, quaver, crotchet, crotchet) - sound upset.
If ‘solo’ is called out one group will continue on their own, duet, two groups, everyone, silence, etc.

To exercise the tongue, place the tongue between your top teeth and closed lips. Circle the tongue around your teeth 8 times clockwise, 8 times anti-clockwise, 4 times clockwise and 4 times anti-clockwise, 2 times and then once similarly. Before doing this exercise say a tongue-twister such as ‘The tip of the tongue, the teeth and the lips, Aaaah’. Repeat again once they have done the tongue exercise.
Say the following Italian words which are all types of pasta: Macaroni, cannelloni; fettuccini, tortellini; capaletti or spaghetti.

Explain that for the voice not to sound ‘tight’ or strained the throat needs to be open and unconstricted. Imagine someone has just given you the most amazing present. As you open the box you take a little inward breath making a silent ‘Ah!’ Feel the cold air at the back of the throat and a very open feeling. Now try the same sensation without taking the in-breath, but still feeling that the throat is really open.
Make the sounds of different consonants and feel where the effort is in the tongue eg ‘tttt’ or ‘dddd’ at the front tip of the tongue; ‘gggg’ or ‘kkkk’ at the back of the tongue.
Make a small face (screw up all the muscles into the middle). On a clap, make the face as big as possible (wide open mouth, eyebrows lifted etc.)

Faces: morphing! Think of contrasting facial expressions – happy/miserable, laughing/crying, shocked/sleepy etc. Imagine the face is a piece of rubber being slowly transformed in slow motion, from the first expression to the second. Change expressions over 8 beats to get really exaggerated expressions; and then quicken to 4 and then 2 beats to get quick, clear reactions. Ask for volunteers to show the group their morphing; the group must name the expressions.
Place fingers on the ‘hinge’ of the jaw and feel the degree of movement when opening and closing the mouth.
Put finger and thumb together and be a busy bee buzzing around. When it is placed on the floor they are silent.
To exercise the tongue imagine they are chewing a very big piece of pink bubblegum with an open mouth, loudly and quickly – feel the lips, tongue and jaw wake up.

Imagine they have just eaten an ice-cream with 100’s and 1000’s all over it which they now have all around their mouth! Lick them off one at a time, going from corner to corner. Try to keep the jaw still to work the tongue.
Yawn out loud first in an exaggerated manner, use hands to stretch out – feel the space inside your mouth.

Speak the following sentence in a dry, monotone voice and then immediately contrast it with a lively, normal delivery. “I went to the Beyonce concert on Saturday and it was the most fantastic night of my life.” Or, “In the last minute of the game Wayne Rooney shot through the centre, dodged two defenders, and scored. It was unbelievable!” Discuss which are the important words and how the speaker would be feeling. How does the listener know they’re feeling that?

Start with ‘Ka’ on beat one of a 4 beat pattern. Then follow this with any high note of their choice to the syllable ‘Ha’ and hold on for 4 beats. Switch from the ‘Ka’ to ‘Ha’ every 4 beats. Then sing a random selection of ‘Ka’s’ changing pitch with each one and using any rhythm of their choice. Then switch to a ‘Ssh’ held on for 8 beats followed by a slow glissando on the syllable ‘Ss’. Rehearse the ‘Ssh’ sound with a crescendo and diminuendo, watching the conductor’s signal. Add to this a random selection of sounds using the syllables ‘Ka’, ‘Too’, ‘Ss’ etc. Finally, watch the conductor for an arm sweep indicating when to come in. On the 2nd arm sweep start to lessen the number of sounds made and on the 3rd arm sweep drop out.
Imagine you are Santa Claus and imitate Santa’s jolly “Ho, ho, ho!” with your voice, first out loud and then silently.

Sing through the alphabet on a monotone with chords I & IV alternating as an accompaniment, with the letter ‘Y’ sung a semitone below with chord V accompanying. Raise up a semitone on each repeat. Gradually get faster.
Lifting of both the soft palate and facial muscles: this can be achieved by telling the pupil to imagine they are taking a bite from the world’s biggest apple – then sing ‘ah’ in this position.
Ask the group to copy you as you sing a mixture of short vocal sounds eg. ff k t as well as longer sounds such as pah.
Practise the words of the song being rehearsed, exaggerating the mouth movements: notice what the mouth-shapes feel like. (Particular good for songs in an unfamiliar language.)

Say the following words in a whisper and then mouthed with no breath.
1. Another and another.
2. Edges blur and intersect, where do all the lines connect?
3. Birds are fish and fish are birds.
4. Cities turning into words.

Sirening is the best vocal warm-up technique. Say ‘Sing’ and hold the ‘ng’ sound. Aiming for no air and not very much volume so that you’re not pushing too much. Sing a sustained sound on one note first then travel up and down the vocal range on this sound as quietly as you can. The sound should be entirely produced out of the nose, not the mouth, so the resonance is in the nose. To test this, hold your nose and the sound should stop!
Say the word ‘laughing’ and then hold on to the ‘ng’ whilst following the conductors hand direction moving up and down.
Think of an animal to draw in the air. Sing the word ‘sing’, holding onto the ‘ng’ whilst drawing this animal. Pitch to rise and fall with their pencil.

Begin by humming ‘mm’ on any pitch. Drop the jaw slightly and ensure lips are relaxed and floppy, so the whole head ‘rings’. Next say the word ‘sing’ and hold the ‘ng’ sound with the mouth slightly open. The feeling is like a laser beam coming out of the top of the head. Breathing should be relaxed and the sound full but not forced. Finally, gradually turn the ‘ng’ into a big, open, steady ‘ah’. Now make an inner smile as you say ‘ah’, raising the cheekbones slightly.
Juggling with your voice (like sirening) but to a ‘Whoah’ sound. Follow the conductors hand for the pitch direction.
Have a large ball and ask two children to bounce/roll/throw it from one to the other. The rest of the group to siren the pitch to ‘Ooo’.
Sing the phrase ‘Fumble fee fumble fee fumble fum’ to a descending pattern. Repeat a semitone higher to the words ‘Kumble kee’, then ‘Tumble tee’ and ‘Bumble Bee’.

Say the following phrases and then ask the group what the vowel sound is in each:
‘Doodle, noodle, poodle, toodle’ - Oo – sing this vowel on a monotone over 8 beats with a crescendo then a diminuendo or make up a vocal pattern starting with 1,2,3,4,5,4,3,2,1.
‘Bosh, dosh, josh, posh, nosh’ – or
‘Sahara, aardvark, car park’ – ah
‘Bear, lair, Clare, rare, mare’ – air
‘Heebie-jeebies, TV freebies’ – ee
Sing up and down a 5 note scale to ‘la’, ‘loo’, ‘see-saw’, ‘bella’, ‘dinner’ & ‘rhubarb’.
Practice the following vowel sounds ‘A’ for ‘apple’, ‘Ee’ for ‘Electricity’, ‘Eh’ for ‘ABC’, ‘O’ for ‘Awful’, ‘O’ for ‘Oh dear’ and ‘Oo’ for ‘Oozing’ on an arpeggio.

Imagine they are in a lift in a big department store starting off in the basement with their lowest voice and then gradually moving up to the 10th floor. Choose a different vowel sound each time and come down.

Ask them to imagine that someone has said to them that they have a 25% chance of winning the National Lottery – do a vocal ‘mmm’ going up and down a little bit. Then they are told that they have a 50% chance of winning – the ‘mmm’ goes up a little further. Then they are told they have a 95% chance of winning – the ‘mmm’ goes up and down even higher. Put all three sounds together.
What might you hear through the window? A police siren; Ambulance (lower); motor bike in the distance getting nearer; birds song.

Imagine there is an elastic band attached to the ceiling with a note on it. They are to pull the elastic band slowly down and then let it go quickly. Start on the sound ‘ng’, then an ‘ee’ then ‘ah’ sound.
Sing up the scale ‘1 2 3 4 5 4 3 2 1’ followed by a clap and the leader singing ‘Move up’ so the scale can be sung again a semitone higher.

Sing slowly up the scale from 1 – 8. Then repeat but ask each child to choose a number and hold on to that number without wavering and being put off by those around them. Repeat the activity but this time change to another number of the scale, singing that number when ‘change’ is called out.
Sing ‘1234543212345432123454321 5 1’. Then repeat a semitone higher and so on. Change the sound to ‘Doo’ then ‘Nah’.
Sing the word ‘Sing’ moving straight on to the ‘ng’ to the notes 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 of the scale then moving up 2, 3, 4, with an ‘ee’ sound on notes 5, 3 and 1.
Sing the word ‘Zinger’ to the notes 5, 4, 3, 2 of the scale finishing with a ‘zing’ on notes 1, 5, 1.
Pretend that you have a little puppy that won’t stop whining. You get fed up with it and throw it out of the window. Fortunately, there is a trampoline below which it bounces up and down on.
Siren up a 5th or an octave to an ‘ng’ sound and then slide down to an ‘Ah’ sound. Repeat moving up a semitone each time.

Sing the notes of the scale to numbers in this progression – 1, 2, 3, (slowly) 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 (quicker) then 1, 8, 5, 3, 1. When confident with this divide the group into 4 parts and give each part a note to hold on to, to form a chord – 8, 5, 3, 1.

With the eyes and mouth closed, whine like a puppy. Keeping that sound, slide slowly but smoothly down to the lowest point of your range and hold. Slide back up in the same fashion. Repeat to a short, repetitive ‘caw’, like a seagull.

Whisper an ‘ah’ to start, with no sound, then add a small amount of sound, to create a very breathy tone. Note that you are using a lot of air. Now gradually focus the tone until you find a highly efficient, slightly harsh but very focused tone, which uses hardly any air at all.
Sing the first 5 notes of the scale going up then down, followed by singing 1 & low 5 to minims followed by a semibreve on 1. Repeat moving up a semitone each time.
Use the rhyme ‘Vizzery, vazzery, vozzery, Vem.’ Split the group into 3 parts. Part 1 sings the words on C, then add Part on E and finally Part 3 on G.

Imagine you are a cowboy on an American ranch about to catch a bull with your lasso. Pretend to throw your lasso, and call out ‘Yee-hi!’ with a high, twangy voice. You catch your imaginary bull first time, and so you punch your fist and call out ‘Yo’ in a deep voice.

Read the following story to the group with them putting in the sound effects: ‘Let me introduce you to some friends of mine who like to go riding. I have a friend called a cowboy. My cowboy says ‘Yee hah’. You say ‘Yee hah’. Match an action to each sound – cowboy throwing a lasso. The cowboy has a girlfriend. The cowboys girlfriend goes ‘Whoo’ in a high pitched voice rising and falling in pitch whilst looking cute with hands clasped under their chin. The cowboy goes ‘Yee hah’ and the girlfriend goes ‘Whoo’. The cowboy has a horse and the horse goes ‘Brrrrrr’ whilst shaking their head. The horse says ‘Brrrrr’, the cowboys girlfriend goes ‘Whoo’ and the cowboy says’ Yee hah’. The cowboy says to his girlfriend ‘Would you like to get to get on my horse?’ And the cowboys girlfriend goes ‘Whoo’. And the horse goes ‘Brrrr’. The cowboys girlfriend gets on the horse. The cowboy gets on the horse and off they go. ‘Shall we?’ Sing a section of the William Tell Overture to ‘Duddle, dah’. Finish by going ‘Whoah!’ Cowboys girlfriend goes ‘Whoo’. Cowboy goes ‘Yee hah’. Cowboy looks around him and sees standing behind him two rather inquisitive but helpful looking gofers. Says to the gofers ‘Where shall we go on my horse?’ ‘Where do you wanna go honey?’ And the gofers helpfully say ‘New York, New York’. (Sing this to A C’ A AG). What do the gofers say? ‘New York, New York’. Cowboys girlfriends goes ‘Whoo’. Horse says ‘Brrrr’. It is a mighty long way to New York so the cowboy gets on the horse. Cowboys girlfriend gets on the horse. They look round and say to the gofers ‘Where is it we’re going?’ The gofers say ‘New York, New York’. So off they go – mime riding the horse whilst singing the tune from the William Tell Overture finishing with ‘Whoah!’ There they arrive at New York. Cowboys girlfriend goes ‘Whoo’ because it’s the first time she has been outside anywhere from where she lives. Cowboy goes ‘Yee hah’ and the horse, very tiredly goes ‘Brrrr’. They look up – there is the Empire State building. Looming behind the Empire State building is the figure of King Kong who says ‘I am King Kong’ (in a deep voice). And the cowboys girlfriend goes ‘Whoo’. The cowboy fancies his chances so says ‘Yee hah’. King Kong says ‘I am King Kong’. Then they look up, right to the very top. King Kong stands there holding in his hand a tiny, tiny kitten. The kitten says ‘Mieow, mieow’. It’s got to be an even higher and tinier kitten. Kitten says ‘Mieow, mieow’. King Kong says ‘I am King Kong’. Cowboys girlfriend goes ‘Whoo’. Cowboy says ‘Yee hah’. Kitten, looking very terrified says ‘Mieow, mieow’. The cowboy gets his lasso and gives it one big hoop. It goes round, another one goes around and he launches it towards the kitten. Tightens the knot and the kitten goes ‘Eeeeh’ in a vocal slide effect finishing with ‘Splat!’ Cowboys girlfriend goes ‘Whoo’. Cowboy goes ‘Yee hah’ because he wouldn’t like to show face in that situation and the rest of us say ‘To be continued’, because that story can run on and on.

‘Space warm-up’: Build up the following parts one by one:
1. Click on the off-beat then say 5, click, 4, click, 3, 2, (click), 1, lift off (click).
2. ‘I can’t hear you, do you read me, (‘kee’), you’re breaking up’ (throat sound ‘kee’ with tongue at the roof of mouth) 1st time all sung on a monotone on the note C. Second time the phrase is sung the tune moves up to a G on the words ‘hear you’ and ‘read me’. Have your hand by your ear as if trying to hear someone.
3. ‘Rock, rock, rock, I’m in a rocket. Rock, rock, rock, lift off’, spoken in a syncopated rhythm and in a posh voice as if they are Prince Charles. Action: swing hips and roll hands moving up.
4. ‘There’s aliens in space (rest), there’s aliens in space, oh yeah!’ spoken in a nasal voice as if they are aliens. Action: Wiggle fingers moving hands down from high to low on the ‘Oh yeah!’ swing both hands up and back by your ears.

Chant ‘left, left’ slowly and step the left foot on the floor. Then move to ‘left, right, left’. Then say ‘Left, right, up in the air’. Then back to ‘left, right, left’. Then ‘left, right, up in the air, left, right, left’. Repeat each one twice before moving on. Then sing ‘Go left, go right, go up in the air, go left, go right, go left’. (F F F F F F F F FF C CD E F). This could be changed to ‘Kick in the air’. Move into ‘Whoa-oh Captain Jack’ (AFF F A) ‘Meet me by the railroad track’ (A F F D F G F). ‘I want to be washed over by the sound of your ‘Whoa-oh’. ‘With some money in your hand …. ‘I’m gonna be a rich man!’ This phrase can be almost spoken finishing with the voice up high. ‘Go left, go left’ (F A F A)….. ‘Go left, right, left’ (F F F F)…. ‘Go left, go right, go up in the air, go left, go right, go left!’ (F F F F F F F F F, F C C D E F) We said ‘Whoa-oh Captain Jack’. Repeat the song again but a semitone higher and with the words ‘With some music in my hands, I’m gonna be a singer man’.

Gently hum five notes up and down (9 notes in all). Change to a vowel and choose a new starting note. Think of phrases or sentences about chocolate to sing to this 9 syllable tune eg. ‘Chocolate is runny when it melts’; ‘Squares are nice but Buttons suit me best’. Invite soloists to sing this in a call & response fashion.

Say the word ‘sing’ and then hum a gentle siren on ‘ng’ from low to high and back down again (check that eyebrows are helping at the upper end!) Place hands on their chest and feel the change in resonance as the voice moves up into head voice and down into chest register. Change this to an open ‘Ah’, ensuring no chins rise and that shoulders and hands are still relaxed.
Speak the words of the warm-up ‘There’s a white van man’ and then sing it coming down the scale.
Say the words ‘Toyota’, ‘Mitsubishi’ and ‘Nissan’ to different pitches.
Sing up and down the scale as follows: 1 121 12321 1234321 etc.
Sing up and down as follows: 1 121 12321 1234321 etc. but remove the number 2 and replace it with a clap.
Then repeat starting at the top with 8 878 87678 8765678 etc. Split the group in two and repeat in canon, with the 2nd group coming in on beat 3 of bar 1, or sing ‘1 121’ and ‘8 878’ simultaneously.

Play ‘Diving board’. Imagine they are on the top board at the swimming pool. Stretch their arm and finger as high as they can to indicate this. Find the highest, gentle note in their voice, and dive off the board, sliding the voice down as they approach the water, using the full range of their voice. Add a deep ‘Splash’ at the end. Imagine you’ve recorded this on a camcorder. Reverse the film, starting with the ‘Splash’ and ending up back on the board.

activities.activity: Bite a Bun / Wear a Wig
activities.description: To the tune of the ‘William Tell’ overture sing the words ‘Bite a bun!’ and then ‘Wear a wig!’ and finally ‘Touch your toes!’ Come up with their own tongue twisters to sing.
To the ‘William Tell Overture’ tune sing the words ‘Checka trade.com’.
To the whole of the ‘William Tell Overture’ tune sing the words ‘Papa’s got a head like a ping pong ball.’
Sing down the scale with the words ‘Fluffy, floppy, puppy’.

In pairs, the first person to go imagines they are an alien from the planet Ning. They speak Ning language which goes like this – demonstrate. This person is going to tell the other person all about their weekend, when they went to see a trampolining competition where all the competitors jumped off a very high cliff, landed on a trampoline at the bottom, one of these super trampolines which can bounce you miles into the air and then drop you back down again. You found all this very exciting, particularly the way they jumped very high and then very low. Then switch over, with the other person being an alien from the planet ‘Zing’. You are going to tell your partner about the pogo stick competition. The amazing thing about these pogo sticks is that the springs are sprung with very springy things and they bounce extremely high.

Exploring vowel sounds: The English language is a complicated language to sing in because the vowel sounds are not all ‘pure’ but many go from one sound to another in what is called a ‘dipthong’. Take each of the 5 vowel sounds and work out which of them is pure and which is not:
A = ‘eh-ee’
E = ‘ee’
I = ‘ah-ee’
O = ‘oh-oo’
U = ‘ee-oo’
When we sing, we want to move to the second of the two sounds as late as possible, otherwise the resulting sound is a very quick, closed sound, which is harder to sing. Practice this with the word ‘high’ – Change quickly: ‘Ha-eee’; Change slowly: ‘Haaaa-ee’. Asks them to identify which one sounds better.

Ask a child for a vowel sound and another child for a pitch and sing a long note on this note for 8 beats. Select another 2 children for a note and vowel sound and repeat. Ask the group how they can make this sound more interesting – move onto the note a 3rd above. Sing two pitches, moving up on my hand signal. Divide the group in half and have one half starting on one note and the other half the other note.
Sing ‘Sing’ going up and down 5 pitches, moving on to the ‘ng’ sound straightaway. Then the dominant and tonic notes again. Move up a semitone.

Voice play where the group copy sounds started by the leader. A quick ‘Whoa’ sliding up, ‘Whee’ coming down, ‘Aah’ (a sigh), ‘mm’ (C E), ‘Did you?’ (quite high pitched voice), ‘Aooh’ sliding down, ‘Hey!’ in a deep voice, ‘Ho!’
Imagine you have a very cute little puppy which someone has locked in a cupboard by mistake. What would it sound like? Then pretend to be a monkey ‘Ooh, ooh, ooh’ in a deepish voice.

Explain how the gaps between notes are called ‘intervals’. Teach the following intervals using the opening of the following well-known songs:
Happy birthday to you = 2nd
Once in royal David’s City = 3rd
A – way in a manger = 4th
Twinkle twinkle little star = 5th
My bon – nie lies over the ocean = 6th
Somewhere o – ver the rainbow = 7th
Some – where over the rainbow = 8th
Play around with some of these opening phrases of the songs to see how well they can sing the intervals. Give a starting note each time and see if the group can sing the next note of the interval given.

Say the sentence ‘I am a nasty witch’ sounding like an evil witch. Sing the phrase on a monotone. Then say the sentence ‘I’ve got a piece of hot potato on my tongue’ in a posh voice. Sing this phrase on a monotone. Remove the words and sing to ‘Aah’ sounding like a witch then with a hot potato sound. Move from one to the other in one sound. Aim to do it keeping the face in the same position letting the inside of the mouth do the work.

Speak the following phrases using different voices eg. sounding bored, angry or posh and in the form of call & response.
I don’t want to sing today, but I will sing anyway.
And if what I sing sounds good, then I’m singing as I should!
Stand up straight, don’t look at the floor!
Lookin’ good and feelin’ fine!
Then march on the spot and sing the words to the American army marching tune.

Using the same tune as above sing these words, changing the type of voice used each time eg. normal; nasal; as if in slow motion moving as if they are up in space; fast and with a high, nasal sound:
We are gonna sing this song, gonna sing this all day long.
This song has got a kickin’ beat, we’re clicking our fingers and tapping our feet.

Explain that the voice has two main registers or sections. The lower part of the voice, often known as the ‘chest’ voice and the higher part of the voice, the ‘head’ voice. As the voice goes higher it needs to make an adjustment, rather like changing gear on a car. Vocally, what needs to happen is that the larynx needs to tilt forward. To make this happen, ask them to imitate the sound of a puppy whining outside a window, wanting you to let him in. Important to keep their chins down whilst doing this.

Play ‘Yoo hoo!’ Ask them to imagine they’ve just spotted their best friend over the other side of the road. Say altogether …. ‘Yoo hoo!’ with their arm in the air. Then answer ‘It’s me’ in their deepest voice with their hand on their chest. Repeat with the other hand. Point out that the top ‘Yoo’ can be turned into a proper note. Sing this.

Sing up and down a 5 note scale, holding onto the 5th note for 3 beats, then come down. Try missing out a note but make sure you hear the missing note in your head with your thinking voice. Then miss out two notes and sing up and down the scale again.
Sing up the scale ‘1 2 3 4 5 4 3 2 1’ to the words ‘Going up the stairs and coming down’, with the scale being sung a semitone higher each time. Change the words to ‘Oo’, ‘Na’, ‘Mah’, ‘Bah’ etc.

‘Yo Yo’. Have an imaginary yo-yo. Say ‘Yo Yo’ sliding down and back up with your voice on the first ‘Yo’ with the 2nd ‘Yo’ being shorter at the top. Then do tricks with your yo-yo eg. flinging it out in front of you and it comes back. Around the world in a circle.

Make the sound of a fly buzzing and the children follow this fly as you move your hand up and down. Then you catch the fly with a clap and say ‘Splat’. Then you open it and go ‘Huh?’ Then you eat it, with a ‘gulp, gulp’ and then you say ‘Mmm’. Then you realise you’ve eaten a fly and you go ‘Yuk!’
The ‘Ghost Exercise’ involves using an ‘oo’ sound to swoop the voice up and down from the very highest point they can squeak to the very lowest point they can reach.

Draw on the IWB various shapes (vocal sound pictures) – curved lines etc. – point to a shape and trace the line. Can they make the shape of the lines with their voices? Sing to ‘oo’, ‘noo’, ‘ee’ or ‘nee’.
Sing up the scale on one syllable followed by octave leaps.

Imagine they are visiting the country of ‘Arkmalia’ where the people are tired all the time. Sing the sentence ‘I like to eat’ to an arpeggio pattern, yawning whilst they sing. Next you visit the country next door called ‘Ekmalia’, where the people are very happy. Sing the sentence again but happily. Then you visit the small country of ‘Ootmoolia’ where the people are very romantic, so sing with their lips forward. Then you visit a country even tinier than Ootmoolia, ‘Eehmelia’ where the people are very tiny. The last country is a large country with very large people in it who love to eat, ‘Okemolia’. Now let’s have a fusion of all our countries together and sing the phase clearly.
Revise singing up and down the 5 note scale. Then try with a complete scale, with a 4 beat rest in the middle for a breath. Then try singing 1,2,1,3,1,4,1,5,5,4,5,3,5,2,5,1.

Sing up and down a major scale or a pentatonic scale. Have half the group sing the first note of the scale and hold it as a drone while the other half sing slowly up the scale to the sound ‘oo’. Listen to the effect then swap over.

Practice a tuning exercise, where the group is divided into 3. Give each group a note to sing from the chord of D. Sing the notes to hum. Encourage them to hold their note whilst being aware of the other two notes of the chord. At a given signal, open this sound out to an ‘Ah’. Encourage the group to drop their jaw and let the tongue fall to the bottom of their mouth.
Sing ‘mmm’ on one note over 4 beats and then open up to ‘maw’. Try any vowel on the end.
Play ‘Bungee jumping’ with the voice. Say ‘Weeeh!’ starting as high in their voice as they can and sliding down as low as their voice will go.

Aim for legato singing. Sing a long phrase from a song they are working on to the sound ‘loo’. As they sing, trace rainbow shapes in the air with their arms, or mime sewing with a very long needle and thread. Then repeat but singing the correct words to the phrase.

To explore their head voice, imagine a piece of string attached to the back of their head. Pull the string out sing a broken arpeggio coming down, then raise up a semitone with another piece of string and so on. Aim to ‘post’ each note clearly through the letter box.
Vowels carry the expression, tone and power in singing. Sing a known song only on the vowels. Only 5 main vowel sounds (ee eh ah oh oo).
Sing a crescendo and diminuendo on each note of a descending scale passage using a range of vowel shapes.

Explain that just as in painting you can create many different colours and shades that express a mood with the human voice, therefore creating a palette of many different vocal ‘colours’. For example:
1. Sing as if you are speaking (called speech quality). You will hear this quality in pop and music theatre songs. If you try to sing too high, however, it will feel very uncomfortable and sound shouted and strained. This is the point where you need to open up the next section of the voice – and allow the larynx to tilt.
2. Tilting makes the sound sweeter. You can make this sound rounder or darker by singing like a politician (or Pavarotti). This quality is often used in classical singing.
3. Pop, country music and music theatre singing also uses a twangy sound. Try singing like a wicked witch or miaow like a cat.
4. For a particular vocal effect you can use a whispered, airy sound, but use this sparingly. Try singing like a princess!

Sing the first 4 bars of ‘Over the Rainbow’ in the following voices:
1. Sing it as grand opera (find the Pavarotti feeling in the diaphragm.)
2. Sing it as the witch in ‘The Wizard of Oz’ (find the twang in the nose.)
3. Sing it as a cockney market trader.
4. Sing it as the Walt Disney princess.
Sing the first line of ‘Somewhere over the rainbow’ rising in pitch a semitone each time.

Prepare a set of cards with the following music notes from the C pentatonic scale: C D E G A and a set of cards containing vocal sounds eg. Ooo mee aah. Put the note cards into a bag and invite a child to pick out between 2 – 5 notes which will make up the tune that the chosen vowel sound will be sung to. Pick the tune out on a tuned percussion instrument.
Sing ‘Vocal percussion’ moving to the right and then left to a slow beat adding finger clicks on the off-beat. Try with ‘b’ ‘d’ ‘t’ and ‘p’.
To encourage legato sounds ask the children to imagine a long piece of chewing gum coming out of the middle of their forehead as they sing, which carries all the words.

activities.activity: Ee to Oh Scale and Whee
activities.description: Sing up the first 4 notes of the scale to ‘ee’ then change the vowel to ‘oh’ for the 5th, 3rd, 4th, 2nd and back to the 1st note. Vary the vowels as they go. To avoid tension, add a clap/’whee’ after each scale (‘whee’ is the falsetto sound you make when sliding down stairs). The ‘whee’ will relax the vocal chords and prevent tension as you go up. As you go higher, take deeper breaths. Focus on the space at the back of the throat (yawn) to prevent a harsh, nasal sound, and keep chin in. To explore the lower range, go down from 5 – 1 of a major scale. Aim to take the bright, clear sound made in the higher register down into the lower notes. As you go lower, check the chin stays in position and doesn’t droop.

Sing the phrase ‘I can sing’ to the notes Bb Bb’ G Eb then B B’ G# E etc. joining the first two notes on smoothly on the word ‘I’. Aim for the octave leap to be like a fast glissando, to what is actually the syllable ‘ah’ with a brief ‘ih’ at the end. Is the whole phrase balanced with the top note not sticking out?
Sing the word ‘Po-pa-ca-ta-pe-tal’ on one note then up and down an 8 note scale. Then repeat but starting with ‘co-‘, ‘o-‘, ‘to-‘, ‘e-‘ and ‘a-‘.
Here are the most common single vowels, ‘a’ (at), ‘ah’ (father), ‘aw’ (awful), ‘ay’ (hate), ‘ee’ (easy), ‘eh’ (every), ‘eye’ (eye), ‘ih’ (it), ‘oh’ (know), ‘oo’ (moon), ‘uh’ (up).
Play ‘Voice walking’ where the class follow one person’s hand going up, down, staying on the same level, to a sound of their choice.

activities.activity_text:
Song 1 – Zoom, Zoom, Zoom (5 mins)


Lyrics

Zoom, zoom, zoom,

We’re going to the moon.

Zoom, zoom, zoom,

We’re going to the moon.


If you want to take a trip,

Climb aboard my rocket ship.

Zoom, zoom, zoom,

We’re going to the moon.


5, 4, 3, 2, 1, blast off.


Actions

Tap steady beat (ta)

Wave scarves for “zoom”

Crouch and jump on “blast off”


Prompt

Can you tap the ta while we sing?

activities.activity: We're Going On A Rocket Ship
activities.description:
ROCKET SHIP – KODÁLY GAME & ACTIVITY IDEAS

 

FOCUS:

Beat, rhythm (ta, ti-ti), steady beat, phrase recognition, movement, inner hearing

 

--------------------------------------------------

 

1. STEADY BEAT ROCKET TAPS

Children tap the steady beat on:

- knees

- floor

- rhythm sticks

 

Teacher sings, children keep the beat.

Extension: swap to silent beat (inner hearing).

 

--------------------------------------------------

 

2. ROCKET SHAPE GAME (SO-MI PREP)

- "Rocket ship" = hands triangle (high space)

- "Moon" = hands circle (rounded shape)

 

Teacher sings:

- Children show shapes only on key words

- Later: teacher sings, children only do actions silently

 

--------------------------------------------------

 

3. ZOOM MOVEMENT GAME (TEMPO + DYNAMICS)

- "Zoom zoom zoom" = forward/back arm movement

- Change tempo:

slow rocket / fast rocket

- Change dynamics:

whisper zoom / loud zoom

 

--------------------------------------------------

 

4. PUT ON YOUR SPACESUIT (SEQUENCE MEMORY GAME)

Children act out:

- spacesuit

- helmet

- seatbelt

 

Game:

- Add actions in order

- Remove teacher support (children recall independently)

 

--------------------------------------------------

 

5. PASS THE ROCKET (BEAT PASSING GAME)

Sit in a circle with an object (rocket toy).

 

- Pass on the beat while singing

- Stop passing on "Zoom zoom zoom"

- Variation: pass only on specific words (e.g. "rocket")

 

--------------------------------------------------

 

6. ECHO SINGING (CALL & RESPONSE)

Teacher: "We’re going on a rocket ship"

Children echo

 

Add:

- actions

- whisper voice

- robot voice

 

--------------------------------------------------

 

7. RHYTHM CLAP (TA / TI-TI INTRO)

Clap rhythm of key phrase:

 

"We’re go-ing on a rock-et ship"

→ introduce syllables:

ta ta ti-ti ta ta

 

Children:

- clap

- speak rhythm

- transfer to sticks

 

--------------------------------------------------

 

8. ROCKET LAUNCH FREEZE GAME

- Move around like rockets

- On "Blast-off!" jump

- Freeze when music stops

 

Extension:

- freeze in rocket shape

activities.activity: Petrol Makes The Racing Car Go
activities.activity_text:
Song 3 – Petrol Makes the Racing Car Go (10 mins)


Lyrics

Petrol makes that racing car go,

Petrol makes that racing car go,

Fill the tank to the top,

Fill the tank to the top,

Petrol makes that racing car go.


Wind makes that sailing boat go,

Wind makes that sailing boat go,

Fill the sails right up,

Fill the sails right up,

Wind makes that sailing boat go.


Coal makes that steam engine go,

Coal makes that steam engine go,

Fill it up, stoke it up,

Fill it up, stoke it up,

Coal makes that steam engine go.


Legs make that bicycle go,

Legs make that bicycle go,

Push the pedals round,

Push the pedals round,

Legs make that bicycle go.


Grass makes that old donkey go,

Grass makes that old donkey go,

Fill his tummy to the top,

Fill his tummy to the top,

Grass makes that old donkey go.


Petrol makes that racing car go,

Fill the tank to the top,

Petrol makes that racing car go.


Actions

Car: drive and tap beat

Boat: sway with scarves

Train: strong chugging (ta)

Bike: pedal legs

Donkey: slow steps


Kodály Link

Say and tap: “ta ta ta ta”

Echo: “ti-ti ti-ti ta”


Prompt

Which one is fast? Which is slow?

Barnacle Bill


When Barnacle Bill was one, he learned to play the drum, over in the clover, half past one.


When Barnacle Bill was two, he learned to buckle his shoe, Bonnie over in the clover, half past one.


When Barnacle Bill was three, he learned to climb a tree.


When Barnacle Bill was four, he learned to scrub the floor.


When Barnacle Bill was five, he learned to swim and dive.

Welcome.description: An action-based song with movements like waving arms, feet, and elbows. Gets kids moving while greeting.

Oh no he's gone to sleep again. I have another song that works but we have to use our hands too.



Sol - Hand like a fist – open the door. (Palm facing yourself)

Mi – I had an ok day splashed by a bus - So so


Hello, Bailey? Hello Bailey? - (Sol and Mi Hand Signals)


“He must be still sleep, Can you help me call for him?” - (Doing hand actions) Bail-ley – repeat –

“No he can’t hear us”. Change - as we learn more signals - and notes.

"I'm going to place Bailey over here so that he can listen. He's a bit shy so it might take a few session for him to join in."
Welcome.activityText: Children explore pitch using hand signals (Sol and Mi) while singing and calling for the puppet Bailey, developing early musical literacy. They also practice listening, following actions, and expressing emotions through gestures, supporting social, communication, and physical development.

We listen to “Engine Engine Number 9” and practise keeping a steady beat using rhythm sticks. We then sing the song together and finish by using bells to play along with a video.


EYFS Areas Covered (Simple)

Communication & Language

  • Listening to the song and following simple instructions.

Physical Development

  • Using rhythm sticks and bells with simple control.

Expressive Arts & Design

  • Joining in with singing and exploring sounds on instruments.
Rhythm Sticks.activityText:
1. Listen & Tap

  3. Sing Together
  4. Children sing along with the song while continuing to tap the beat.
  5. Play Bells
  6. Children are given bells and try to play along with the video.

If she’s polished, won’t she shine?

Engine, engine number nine,

All aboard!

Rhythm Sticks.activity: This is the way to make Pizza
Rhythm Sticks.description:
1. Roll the Dough

Action: Roll the dough with sticks.


This is the way we (roll the dough) – repeat 3 times

This is the way we roll the dough, roll the dough together


2. Stir the Sauce

Action: Stir the sauce with sticks.


This is the way we (stir the sauce) – repeat 3 times

This is the way we stir the sauce, stir the sauce together


3. Spread the Sauce

Action: Spread the sauce with sticks.


This is the way we (spread the sauce) – repeat 3 times

This is the way we spread the sauce, spread the sauce together


4. Add Toppings

Prompt: Now we've got the dough and the sauce, it’s time for some toppings.

Ask the children: “What do you like on your pizza?”


5. Chop the Ham

Action: Chop ham with sticks.


This is the way we (chop the ham) – repeat 3 times

This is the way we chop the ham, chop the ham together


6. Grate the Cheese

Action: Rub sticks together to imitate grating cheese.


This is the way we grate the cheese


7. Place the Basil

Action: Tap sticks gently in front of you to place basil.


This is the way we place the basil
