lesson2.title: test
lesson2.introduction:
Introduce the dinosaur characters previously explored in the music lesson. Recap the meaning of a “whoosh” and practise using it to move the circle out safely and quickly.

Invite the children to move like different dinosaurs:

T-Rex – tall, strong, powerful movements

Long-neck dinosaurs – slow, stretched, reaching movements

Ask children to recall facts about the dinosaurs, including whether they eat meat or vegetables.

Warm-Up: Whoosh Practice (5 minutes)

Practise using the whoosh several times to:

Move the circle in and out

Develop focus and listening

Prepare the group for transitions within the drama

  • Trees and branches Trees and branches
  • Trees and branches
  • LeavesLeaves
  • Leaves
  • Large rocksLarge rocks
  • Large rocks
  • Long grassLong grass
  • Long grass

Rhythm Sticks.activityText: Children use sticks to imitate cooking actions like rolling, stirring, spreading, chopping, grating, and tapping, developing fine and gross motor skills. They also follow repetitive songs and prompts, supporting listening, sequencing, and social participation.

Rhythm Sticks.description:
Take a stick in this hand,

Take a stick in that.

Lay them both together,

Tap, tap, tap.


Tap them to the ceiling,

Tap them to the floor,

Tap them just in front of you,

And tap them on your knees.               (Sticks remain on knees until T continues)


Tap them to the ceiling,                        (Sung Slowly)

Tap them to the floor,                            (Sung Slowly)

Tap them just in front of you,                (Sung Quickly)

And tap them on your knees.               (Sticks remain on knees until T continues)


Tap them fast, tap them slow,

Tap them high, tap them low.

Tap them fast, tap them slow,

And place them on your knees. (Slowly)


(Repeat first verse, though slow down towards the end)

Take a stick in this hand,

Take a stick in that.

Lay them both together,

Let them tap, tap, tap. (Sticks remain on knees until T continues)
Rhythm Sticks.activityText: Children use sticks to follow directional and tempo instructions, developing coordination, rhythm, and fine motor control. They also practice listening carefully, copying actions, and adjusting speed and movement, supporting musical awareness and concentration.

We're going to pretend that this is my chewed-up bubble gum... Urgh


(Mime blowing)

POP!

Oh no – it's all sticky!


🎵

Icky sticky, icky sticky bubble gum,

Bubble gum, bubble gum,

Icky sticky, icky sticky bubble gum...

And it’s stuck to my... shoulder!

So we pull...

And we pull...

And we pull pull it away!

Till they pop! Till they pop!

We listen to “Engine Engine Number 9” and practise keeping a steady beat using rhythm sticks. We then sing the song together and finish by using bells to play along with a video.


EYFS Areas Covered (Simple)

Communication & Language

  • Listening to the song and following simple instructions.

Physical Development

  • Using rhythm sticks and bells with simple control.

Expressive Arts & Design

  • Joining in with singing and exploring sounds on instruments.
Rhythm Sticks.activityText:

  2. Children listen to Engine Engine Number 9 and tap rhythm sticks to the beat.
  3. Sing Together
  4. Children sing along with the song while continuing to tap the beat.
  5. Play Bells
  6. Children are given bells and try to play along with the video.


Engine, engine number nine,

Going down Chicago line,

If she’s polished, won’t she shine?

Engine, engine number nine,

All aboard!

Kodaly Songs.activityText:
Apple Tree is a gem. . . that's the best way I can describe it. It's got a rhythm that is easy for my 1st graders to derive, is great for teaching so-mi (extracted in the first and third phrases), is great for la (extracted in the 2nd) and for do (the final phrase of the song). What's more is it has a fun game that the kids ask for over and over and over and over and over. . . . . and over again for. If you're not familiar with the song, here it is:




The game:

Formation: standing circle, holding hands, with two people (I use myself and a student volunteer) making and arch.


Action:  as the song is sung, the students process in a circular motion with the circle going under the arch formed by the teacher and student volunteer. On the word "out" the arch lowers their arms, catching whoever is under the tree at that time. The caught student then joins the arch (I refer to it as the tree). The game is repeated until all the "apples" (the students in the walking circle) are caught. As the tree grows, more students are caught. The students love this!


As I mentioned, this song is GREAT for 1st and 2nd grades. I use it for ta & ti-ti reading and so-mi-la reading in the first grade. I have created sets of tone ladders that the students use to sing the phrases. After they have derived the known solfa (the first three phrases of the song), they sing it while pointing to the solfége apples on their tone ladder.

Kodaly Songs.description:
Come and join the march with me.

It is easy you can see.

One foot up and one foot down.

Marching, Marching through the town.

Parachute Games.description:
Jelly On A Plate

Jelly on a plate jelly on a plate Wibble wobble wibble wobble
Jelly on a plate.

Jelly on your head - jelly on your head etc.
Jelly on your hands - jelly in your hands
Jelly on your knees - jelly on your tummy
Jelly on your tummy - jelly on your knees
Jelly in your mouth - jelly in your mouth
Eat it up eat it up
Jelly in your mouth.

Great for babies and children of all ages.   Songs such as 5 little monkeys jumping on the bed can be done by placing teddies on the parachute, singing and then bouncing one off at a time. Babies can be placed underneath the parachute whilst practitioners gently move it up and down singing songs. Also songs such as the grand old duke of york are good to do while holding onto the parachute.

lesson1.title: A Trip To The Gallery
lesson1.mainActivity:
Learning Outcomes

By the end of the lesson:


Lesson Structure

1. Warm-Up Game – Pass the Emotion (10 minutes)

Purpose:

Builds listening, quick thinking, emotional expression and confidence speaking aloud.

Children stand in a circle.

Teacher starts by turning to the child next to them and saying:

“I’m going to the match and I feel… excited!”

(Using big facial expression and gesture.)

That child copies the feeling and passes a new emotion on:

“I’m going to the match and I feel… nervous!”

Continue around the circle.

Encourage varied vocabulary:

  • excited
  • proud
  • worried
  • cross
  • tired
  • cold
  • impatient
  • hopeful

Challenge (Year 2):

Add a reason:

“I feel nervous because it’s my first match.”


✔ No pressure

✔ Keep pace lively

✔ Celebrate expressive faces and voices


2. Revisiting the Painting – What Happened Before? (10 minutes)

Show the painting again.

Ask:

  • Are they nearly there?
  • Have they just left home?
  • Has something happened already?
  • Did everyone want to go?

Introduce the idea:

“Today we are going to show what happened before this picture.”

Children discuss in pairs:

“What might have happened at home?”

Collect ideas:

  • Someone couldn’t find their ticket
  • A child didn’t want to go
  • A team scarf was missing
  • Someone was late
  • They argued about which team would win
  • The baby needed feeding
  • It was raining

Teacher models a short example scene (30 seconds) with simple dialogue:

“I can’t find my scarf!”

“Well hurry up — we’ll be late!”

Keep it simple and achievable.

3. Main Activity – Creating the “Before” Scene (25 minutes)

Children stay in the same small groups as previous lessons.

Task:

Create a short scene that shows what happened just before the painting.

Structure support (very important for KS1):

  1. Beginning – What is the problem?
  2. Middle – What happens?
  3. End – They leave for the match.

Rules:

  • Everyone must speak at least once.
  • Stay in role.
  • Show your feeling clearly.
  • Keep it short (30–60 seconds).

Teacher Support Prompts

  • Who are you?
  • How do you feel?
  • What has gone wrong?
  • Can the audience see your emotion?
  • Can you say it clearly?

Differentiation

Support (Year 1):

  • Provide sentence stems:“I can’t find my…”“I feel…”“Let’s go!”
  • “I can’t find my…”
  • “I feel…”
  • “Let’s go!”
  • Allow paired lines.
  • Keep dialogue very short.

Challenge (Year 2):

  • Include contrasting emotions in the group.
  • Add two lines of natural back-and-forth dialogue.
  • Show status (Who is in charge?)

4. Performance – Beginning to Middle to End (10–12 minutes)

Create a clear performance space.

Audience expectations:

  • Quiet bodies
  • Listening ears
  • Kind words

Each group performs.

After each performance, ask:

  • What was the problem?
  • How did it get solved?
  • How were they feeling at the start?
  • Did anyone change emotion?

Encourage specific vocabulary beyond “happy/sad.”

5. Plenary – Linking All Three Lessons (5 minutes)

Sit in a circle.

Ask:

  • Was it easier to freeze or to speak?
  • What helps you stay in role?
  • How did working as a group feel today?

Revisit progression:

  • Lesson 1 – Freeze-frame
  • Lesson 2 – Bringing it to life
  • Lesson 3 – Creating what happened before


Key Vocabulary


Role

Scene

Problem

Beginning

Middle

End

Emotion

Dialogue

Audience

Assessment Opportunities

✔ Can pupils sequence a simple scene?

✔ Can they speak clearly in role?

✔ Do they listen and respond to others?

✔ Can they show emotional change?

Teacher Reflection Prompt

  • Did pupils understand the idea of “before”?
  • Were scenes manageable and structured?
  • Did Year 2 pupils extend dialogue naturally?
  • Are children now ready to explore what happens after the match in Lesson 4?
lesson1.learningOutcome:
Year 1

  • I can pretend to be a character.
  • I can show how my character feels.
  • I can say one short sentence in role.
  • I can take turns when speaking.

Year 2

  • I can stay in role during a short scene.
  • I can explain how my character feels and why.
  • I can use more than one sentence in role.
  • I can build on what someone else says in a scene.

KS2 Songs.description:
Canon in the snow

Phoo! Brrr! Phoo! Brrr!

Gosh it’s flippin’ cold it’s really cold

It’s freezin’, it’s really freezin’

 I can’t feel my toes or fingertips.

 Get a bag o’ chips, that’ll warm you up, John.

 Ah! What? No! What?

 Chip shop’s closed. Oh!

 Never mind, no sweat, keep your hair on.

 Let’s go down the old embankment:

 Nice cup o’ tea from the lady with peroxide hair.

She never talks, never smiles, never winks, never nothin’.

Refrain

I come a zim-ba, zim-ba za-ya,

I come a zim-ba, zim-ba zay.

(last time)

Verse

See him there, the Zulu warrior,

See him there, the Zulu chief, chief, chief.

See him — chief, chief, chief.

Yo!

lesson1.title: Exploring Rhythm and Beat
lesson1.plenary:
Performance circle: Each pair shares their created rhythm pattern. Class tries to copy it back.

Exit question: "What is the difference between beat and rhythm?"
lesson1.resources:
• Rhythm cards (sets of 8)
  • Untuned percussion instruments
  • Audio: "We Will Rock You" by Queen
  • Interactive whiteboard for visual patterns
lesson1.assessment:
Observe children during activities - can they maintain the beat independently?

Use exit tickets: Draw your favourite rhythm pattern
lesson1.vocabulary:
Beat - The steady pulse in music
Rhythm - The pattern of long and short sounds
Tempo - How fast or slow the beat is
Pattern - A sequence that repeats
lesson1.introduction:
Begin by playing a familiar song and ask children to clap along. Discuss: "What are we clapping? Is it the same all the way through?"

Introduce the concept of a steady beat like a heartbeat - always the same speed.
lesson1.keyQuestions:
• Can you feel the beat in this music?
  • Is the rhythm the same as the beat?
  • How many beats are in your pattern?
  • What happens if we speed up the tempo?
lesson1.mainActivity:
Activity 1: Beat vs Rhythm

Play "We Will Rock You" - demonstrate the steady beat (stomp stomp clap) vs the rhythm of the words.

Activity 2: Rhythm Patterns

Using rhythm cards, children practice 4-beat patterns in pairs. Progress from copying to creating.
lesson1.differentiation:
Support: Use visual rhythm cards with symbols, pair with confident partner

Challenge: Create 8-beat patterns, add dynamics (loud/quiet)
lesson1.learningOutcome:
Children will be able to:

  • Maintain a steady beat when clapping or playing instruments
  • Identify the difference between rhythm and beat
  • Create simple 4-beat rhythm patterns
lesson1.successCriteria:
I can:

  • Keep a steady beat for at least 8 counts
  • Copy a simple rhythm pattern
  • Create my own rhythm using body percussion

Core Songs.description:
Circle Chat

  • What is a robot?
  • What do you know about robots?
  • What do they look like?
  • How do they move?
  • What can/can’t they do?


Warm up


  • T models saying the lyrics in different ways/pitches.
  • Children can share their version too.


“I am a robot - I go beep-beep-beep beep-bop”

  • Child chosen to lead. Everyone has to copy their movement – they can only use their head, neck, arms and hands – stay seated.


Robot Dance


  • During ‘This is my robot dance’ section, one or two children become ‘robots’ and dance in the middle of the circle to show off their robot moves!
  • Each time the ‘I am a robot’ section returns, everyone stands still and does the robot moves on the spot, copying the leader.
  • Select new robots to dance in the middle of the circle each time the ‘This is my robot dance’ section returns.


Possible extension


  • Move like a robot activity with piano – slow, quick.
  • https://www.bbc.co.uk/cbeebies/watch/lets-go-club-robot-freeze-game

Hot Cross Buns

Hot cross buns! Hot cross buns!

One a penny, two a penny, Hot cross buns!


If you have no daughters, Give them to your sons.

One a penny, two a penny, Hot cross buns!

General Game.description: Moving and stopping to the beat responding to speeds, levels and actions

Nautical Commands Game - Check Canva Link


Port — Run to the left of the playing space

Starboard — Run to the right

Bow — Run to the front

Stern — Run to the back

Shark! — Put your hand on your head like a fin

Captain on Deck — Stand to attention and salute

Land Ahoy! — Shade your eyes and point to the horizon

Scrub the decks — Crouch down and pretend to clean the floor

Climb the rigging — Pretend to climb a ladder

Man the lifeboats — Sit on the floor and pretend to row

Rats! — Creep around squeaking

Seagull — Duck and cover your head

Man overboard! — Pretend to splash around or swim

Welcome.description: Children sit on the floor in a circle. The teacher rolls the ball to child, they sing/say their name as they catch the ball.

Hello Everyone

Hello Everyone

Hello Everyone

It's time for music now!


(Chords - C, G, F)


Change to jum, march on the spot (slowly, quickly), clap with me, spin around.


Hello - first child's name then continue through the group.

Finally - "Hello Bailey? - oh no. I think he's asleep".

Let's sing it again (louder, quicker, slower, quieter) and see if we can wake him up.

"He's a bit shy - he will get better as he get used to you. Let's pop him in my chair, he likes to listed".
Welcome.activityText: Children practice listening, taking turns, and responding to peers’ names while singing and moving to music. They develop gross motor skills through actions like marching, jumping, clapping, and spinning, while exploring voice and rhythm.

Welcome.description: An interactive call-and-response hello song with actions like stomping feet, dancing, and playing a beat.

If you have no daughters, Give them to your sons.

One a penny, two a penny, Hot cross buns!

Verse 1 – Elephant


Down in the jungle where nobody goes,

There’s a great grey elephant washing his clothes.

With a rub-a-dub here and a rub-a-dub there,

That’s how an elephant washes his clothes!

He goes stomp, stomp, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes stomp, stomp, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes stomp, stomp, a boogie boogie woogie —

That’s how an elephant washes his clothes!


Verse 2 – Monkey


Down in the jungle where nobody goes,

There’s a cheeky little monkey washing his clothes.

With a rub-a-dub here and a rub-a-dub there,

That’s how a monkey washes his clothes!

He goes ooo ooo, ah ah, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes ooo ooo, ah ah, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes ooo ooo, ah ah, a boogie boogie woogie —

That’s how a monkey washes his clothes!


Verse 3 – Snake


Down in the jungle where nobody goes,

There’s a slithering snake washing his clothes.

With a rub-a-dub here and a rub-a-dub there,

That’s how a slithering snake washes his clothes!

He goes siss, siss, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes siss, siss, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes siss, siss, a boogie boogie woogie —

That’s how a slithering snake washes his clothes!


Verse 4 – Crocodile


Down in the jungle where nobody goes,

There’s a happy snappy crocodile washing his clothes.

With a rub-a-dub here and a rub-a-dub there,

That’s how a crocodile washes his clothes!

He goes snap, snap, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes snap, snap, a boogie boogie woogie,

He goes snap, snap, a boogie boogie woogie —

That’s how a crocodile washes his clothes!

Teaching Units.activityText:
Dandelion Clocks


Chorus


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Tick, tock, tick, tock.


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Let’s blow the time away!


(“Blow” is spoken)



Verse 1


(Blow) It’s one o’clock,


(Blow) It’s two o’clock,


(Blow) It’s three o’clock,


Let’s blow the time away!



Chorus


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Tick, tock, tick, tock.


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Let’s blow the time away!



Verse 2


(Blow) It’s four o’clock,


(Blow) It’s five o’clock,


(Blow) It’s six o’clock,


Let’s blow the time away!



Chorus


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Tick, tock, tick, tock.


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Let’s blow the time away!



Verse 3


(Blow) It’s seven o’clock,


(Blow) It’s eight o’clock,


(Blow) It’s nine o’clock,


Let’s blow the time away!



Chorus


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Tick, tock, tick, tock.


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Let’s blow the time away!



Verse 4


(Blow) It’s ten o’clock,


(Blow) It’s eleven o’clock,


(Blow) It’s twelve o’clock,


Let’s blow the time away!



Chorus x2


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Tick, tock, tick, tock.


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Let’s blow the time away!


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Tick, tock, tick, tock.


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Let’s blow the time away!

Chinese New Year


Chinese New Year is here again,

here again, here again


Chinese New Year is here again

chunjie, chunjie, chunjie (‘sunyay’)


Look at the dragon breathing flames ...

lung, lung, lung (‘loong’)


Crackers are banging in the street …

baozhu, baozhu, baozhu (‘powd-zoo’)


Children have packets of money to spend …

qian, qian, qian (‘tee-en’)


Everyone dances through the streets …

wu, wu, wu (‘woo’)

Plenary.description: Each group performs their three frames. The audience identifies the moment of greatest tension and one strong storytelling choice.

Warm-Up.description: Pupils work in pairs facing each other. One leads, the other mirrors every movement. Switch roles after 90 seconds. Builds focus, eye contact, and ensemble awareness.

Main Activity.description: Groups of four create three still images that retell a familiar fable (e.g. The Tortoise and the Hare). Encourage clear use of levels, facial expression, and gesture.

orderedActivities.description: Pupils work in pairs facing each other. One leads, the other mirrors every movement. Switch roles after 90 seconds. Builds focus, eye contact, and ensemble awareness.
Groups of four create three still images that retell a familiar fable (e.g. The Tortoise and the Hare). Encourage clear use of levels, facial expression, and gesture.
Each group performs their three frames. The audience identifies the moment of greatest tension and one strong storytelling choice.

Plenary.description: Two pairs share their strongest scene. Class identifies the inciting moment and a clear ending.

Warm-Up.description: In pairs, pupils build a short scene by always accepting and extending their partner's offer. Stop after a minute and reset with a new "where".

Main Activity.description: Pairs improvise a two-minute scene around a "secret" only one character knows. Run twice, then perform best take to a partner pair.

orderedActivities.description: In pairs, pupils build a short scene by always accepting and extending their partner's offer. Stop after a minute and reset with a new "where".
Pairs improvise a two-minute scene around a "secret" only one character knows. Run twice, then perform best take to a partner pair.
Two pairs share their strongest scene. Class identifies the inciting moment and a clear ending.

Warm-Up.activity: Active Listening: Rhythms of West Africa
Warm-Up.description: Play a short djembe ensemble recording. Pupils tap the underlying pulse, then identify the call-and-response pattern and shifting layers.

Composition.description: Pairs invent their own 4-beat ostinato using two body sounds. Notate using picture symbols on whiteboards. Perform in a circle with each pair adding in turn.

Main Activity.description: Teach a four-bar body percussion ostinato (stomp, clap, click, clap). Split the class into three groups and stagger entries to build a textured groove.

orderedActivities.activity: Active Listening: Rhythms of West Africa
orderedActivities.description: Play a short djembe ensemble recording. Pupils tap the underlying pulse, then identify the call-and-response pattern and shifting layers.
Teach a four-bar body percussion ostinato (stomp, clap, click, clap). Split the class into three groups and stagger entries to build a textured groove.
Pairs invent their own 4-beat ostinato using two body sounds. Notate using picture symbols on whiteboards. Perform in a circle with each pair adding in turn.

Theory.description: Pupils notate one of their improvised melodies onto a 4-bar treble stave. Peer-check note placement.

Plenary.description: Volunteers perform their notated melody from the score while the rest of the class follows along.

Main Activity.description: Using glockenspiels with only the C-D-E-G-A bars in place, pupils improvise short call-and-response melodies over a 4-bar drone.

orderedActivities.description: Using glockenspiels with only the C-D-E-G-A bars in place, pupils improvise short call-and-response melodies over a 4-bar drone.
Pupils notate one of their improvised melodies onto a 4-bar treble stave. Peer-check note placement.
Volunteers perform their notated melody from the score while the rest of the class follows along.

Warm-Up.description: Standing in a circle, lead pupils through a head-to-toe joint mobilisation set to gentle rhythmic music. Encourage controlled breathing.

Choreography.description: In pairs, pupils invent a 4-count movement motif using one travel, one shape, and one gesture. Repeat × 4 to a steady count.

Main Activity.description: Pairs join into fours and perform their motifs in canon (overlapping by one count). Reflect on how the unison sections feel different to the canon sections.

orderedActivities.description: Standing in a circle, lead pupils through a head-to-toe joint mobilisation set to gentle rhythmic music. Encourage controlled breathing.
In pairs, pupils invent a 4-count movement motif using one travel, one shape, and one gesture. Repeat × 4 to a steady count.
Pairs join into fours and perform their motifs in canon (overlapping by one count). Reflect on how the unison sections feel different to the canon sections.

Plenary.description: Each group performs. Audience offers one "glow" (something that worked) and one "grow" (something to develop).

Warm-Up.description: Pupils travel around the space embodying contrasting characters (royalty, scientist, athlete). Freeze on the cymbal crash and hold the character's posture.

Choreography.description: Groups of five compose a 32-count piece contrasting frenetic "storm" and held "stillness" sections. Use shape, level, and dynamic clearly.

orderedActivities.description: Pupils travel around the space embodying contrasting characters (royalty, scientist, athlete). Freeze on the cymbal crash and hold the character's posture.
Groups of five compose a 32-count piece contrasting frenetic "storm" and held "stillness" sections. Use shape, level, and dynamic clearly.
Each group performs. Audience offers one "glow" (something that worked) and one "grow" (something to develop).

Teddy Bear, Teddy Bear, climb the stairs

Teddy Bear, Teddy Bear, switch off the light

Teddy Bear, Teddy Bear, say goodnight
Children cover their face with their hands for “Big black clouds, then uncover their hands for the “Mr. Sun”.

Students form a circle. Teacher bounces a ball in the middle and bounces it to one child when their name is sung at the end. That student goes into the center to bounce the ball for the next verse and chooses another name to sing at the end.

Begin with sticks in front of you and simply follow the words  Take a stick in this hand, Take a stick in that.  Lay them both together, Let them tap, tap, tap.  Tap them to the ceiling, Tap them to the floor, Tap them just in front of you , And place them on the floor.

Wind the bobbin up, wind the bobbin up, Pull, pull, tap tap tap. Wind it back again, wind it back again, Pull, pull, tap tap tap.  Point to the ceiling, point to the floor,  Point to the window, Point to the door.  Tap your sticks together, one, two, three. Put your sticks upon your knees.

We’re gonna play and play and play and stop.

Play and play and play and stop.

Play and play and play and stop.

Then we’ll play some more.

 

Repeat. (Tap floor, tap in front, tap a partner, tap knees).

Verse 1 – Rocket 🚀 (arm zooming upwards)


On Bonfire Night at five o’clock,

I took my money to the firework shop.

I bought a rocket, it cost one pence,

I put my rocket by the fence,

And my rocket went...


🎶 Whooooooosh! (arm shooting up high)

Verse 2 – Whizzer 🎡 (twirling finger round)

On Bonfire Night at five o’clock,

I took my money to the firework shop.

I bought a whizzer, it cost two pence,

I put my whizzer by the fence,

And my whizzer went...


🎶 Whiz – Whiz – Whiz – Whiz – Whiz! (spin hands round and round)

🎶 And my rocket went… Whoooooosh!


Rip-Rap 💥 (hand in arch on other hand)

On Bonfire Night at five o’clock,

I took my money to the firework shop.

I bought a rip-rap, it cost three pence,

I put my rip-rap by the fence,

And my rip-rap went...

]

🎶 Rip – Rap – Rip – Rap – Rip! (sharp claps on each “rap”)

🎶 And my whizzer went… Whiz – Whiz – Whiz…

🎶 And my rocket went… Whoooooosh!


Fountain 🎇 (fingers wiggling upwards like sparks)

On Bonfire Night at five o’clock,

I took my money to the firework shop.

I bought a fountain, it cost four pence,

I put my fountain by the fence,

And my fountain went...


🎶 Whoosh – Whoosh – Whoosh – Whoosh – Whoosh! (jazz hands rising)

🎶 And my rip-rap went… Rip – Rap – Rip – Rap – Rip!

🎶 And my whizzer went… Whiz – Whiz – Whiz…

🎶 And my rocket went… Whoooooosh!

One child (Grandma) faces away at end. Others sneak forward. When Grandma turns, everyone freezes! If caught moving, return to line. • Builds body control and suspense • Develops spatial awareness • Creates tension and dramatic movement

Students are given a random, everyday object (e.g. a shoe, a bottle, a piece of string). In small groups, they must create a short play where the object is the central character or prop. • Encourages creative use of props • Develops imaginative storytelling and problem-solving • Builds the skill of creating meaning with simple objects

Circle Chat

  • What is a robot?
  • What do you know about robots?
  • What do they look like?
  • How do they move?
  • What can/can’t they do?


Warm up


  • T models saying the lyrics in different ways/pitches.
  • Children can share their version too.


“I am a robot - I go beep-beep-beep beep-bop”

  • Child chosen to lead. Everyone has to copy their movement – they can only use their head, neck, arms and hands – stay seated.


Robot Dance


  • During ‘This is my robot dance’ section, one or two children become ‘robots’ and dance in the middle of the circle to show off their robot moves!
  • Each time the ‘I am a robot’ section returns, everyone stands still and does the robot moves on the spot, copying the leader.
  • Select new robots to dance in the middle of the circle each time the ‘This is my robot dance’ section returns.


Possible extension


  • Move like a robot activity with piano – slow, quick.
  • https://www.bbc.co.uk/cbeebies/watch/lets-go-club-robot-freeze-game

Bubble, bubbles Everywhere

Bubbles, bubbles in the air

Bubbles, bubbles floating down

Bubble. bubbles hit the ground

Verse 1 – Rocket 🚀 (arm zooming upwards)


On Bonfire Night at five o’clock,

I took my money to the firework shop.

I bought a rocket, it cost one pence,

I put my rocket by the fence,

And my rocket went...


🎶 Whooooooosh! (arm shooting up high)

Verse 2 – Whizzer 🎡 (twirling finger round)

On Bonfire Night at five o’clock,

I took my money to the firework shop.

I bought a whizzer, it cost two pence,

I put my whizzer by the fence,

And my whizzer went...


🎶 Whiz – Whiz – Whiz – Whiz – Whiz! (spin hands round and round)

🎶 And my rocket went… Whoooooosh!


Rip-Rap 💥 (hand in arch on other hand)

On Bonfire Night at five o’clock,

I took my money to the firework shop.

I bought a rip-rap, it cost three pence,

I put my rip-rap by the fence,

And my rip-rap went...

]

🎶 Rip – Rap – Rip – Rap – Rip! (sharp claps on each “rap”)

🎶 And my whizzer went… Whiz – Whiz – Whiz…

🎶 And my rocket went… Whoooooosh!

I took my money to the firework shop.

I put my fountain by the fence,

And my fountain went...


🎶 Whoosh – Whoosh – Whoosh – Whoosh – Whoosh! (jazz hands rising)

🎶 And my rip-rap went… Rip – Rap – Rip – Rap – Rip!

🎶 And my whizzer went… Whiz – Whiz – Whiz…

🎶 And my rocket went… Whoooooosh!

On Bonfire Night at five o’clock,

And my banger went...

🎶 And my fountain went… Whoosh – Whoosh – Whoosh…

🎶 And my rip-rap went… Rip – Rap – Rip – Rap – Rip!

🎶 And my whizzer went… Whiz – Whiz – Whiz…

🎶 And my rocket went… Whoooooosh!


Roman Candle 🕯️ (hands pulsing out like bursts of light)

On Bonfire Night at five o’clock,

I took my money to the firework shop.

I bought a Roman Candle, it cost six pence,

I put my candle by the fence,

🎶 Phut – Phut – Phut – Phut – Phut!

activities.description: A cheerful song sung to the tune of 'My Bonnie Lies Over the Ocean' about bubbles floating over flowers, trees, and grass. Children sing about watching pretty bubbles popping all around them.
activities.activityText:
The bubbles flew over the flowers.
The bubbles flew over the trees.
The bubbles flew over the grass.
So many bubbles, I see.
Bubbles, bubbles
As pretty as bubbles can be.
Bubbles, bubbles
Popping all around me.

activities.activity: I'm Going to Blow a Bubble
activities.description: An interactive action song where children cup their hands around their mouths and pretend to blow a bubble, opening their hands wider as the bubble grows, then clapping to pop it. Perfect for teaching about growing and stopping.
activities.activityText:
I'm going to blow a bubble.
Watch me while I blow.
See it's getting bigger
Watch it grow and grow.
Do you think it's big enough?
Maybe I should stop.
For if I give another blow
My bubble may POP!

Actions: Have children cup hands around their mouths, then open them wider as they blow. Then clap their hands together at the end to pop the bubble.

Students form a circle. Teacher bounces a ball in the middle and bounces it to one child when their name is sung at the end. That student goes into the center to bounce the ball for the next verse and chooses another name to sing at the end.

activities.activity: On a little piece of string
activities.description: Teacher puts out a straight line of string in the centre of the circle. One child is chosen to be the elephant. They step along the string, one foot after the other. At the end of the song they call another child to come and walk along the string.

Sol/Mi - Song and Game/s  Song  Bee, bee, bumble bee, Stung a man upon his knee. Stung a pig upon his snout, I declare that you are out!  Game  This rhyme is often accompanied by a circle game where children pass an object around while singing. The child holding the object on the word out is then out of the game.

Nautical Commands Game - Check Canva Link


Port — Run to the left of the playing space

Starboard — Run to the right

Bow — Run to the front

Stern — Run to the back

Shark! — Put your hand on your head like a fin

Captain on Deck — Stand to attention and salute

Land Ahoy! — Shade your eyes and point to the horizon

Scrub the decks — Crouch down and pretend to clean the floor

Climb the rigging — Pretend to climb a ladder

Man the lifeboats — Sit on the floor and pretend to row

Rats! — Creep around squeaking

Seagull — Duck and cover your head

Man overboard! — Pretend to splash around or swim

Oh no he's gone to sleep again. I have another song that works but we have to use our hands too.



Sol - Hand like a fist – open the door. (Palm facing yourself)

Mi – I had an ok day splashed by a bus - So so


Hello, Bailey? Hello Bailey? - (Sol and Mi Hand Signals)


“He must be still sleep, Can you help me call for him?” - (Doing hand actions) Bail-ley – repeat –

“No he can’t hear us”. Change - as we learn more signals - and notes.

"I'm going to place Bailey over here so that he can listen. He's a bit shy so it might take a few session for him to join in."
activities.activityText: Children explore pitch using hand signals (Sol and Mi) while singing and calling for the puppet Bailey, developing early musical literacy. They also practice listening, following actions, and expressing emotions through gestures, supporting social, communication, and physical development.

activities.description:
Take a stick in this hand,

Take a stick in that.

Lay them both together,

Tap, tap, tap.


Tap them to the ceiling,

Tap them to the floor,

Tap them just in front of you,

And tap them on your knees.               (Sticks remain on knees until T continues)


Tap them to the ceiling,                        (Sung Slowly)

Tap them to the floor,                            (Sung Slowly)

Tap them just in front of you,                (Sung Quickly)

And tap them on your knees.               (Sticks remain on knees until T continues)


Tap them fast, tap them slow,

Tap them high, tap them low.

Tap them fast, tap them slow,

And place them on your knees. (Slowly)


(Repeat first verse, though slow down towards the end)

Take a stick in this hand,

Take a stick in that.

Lay them both together,

Let them tap, tap, tap. (Sticks remain on knees until T continues)
activities.activityText: Children use sticks to follow directional and tempo instructions, developing coordination, rhythm, and fine motor control. They also practice listening carefully, copying actions, and adjusting speed and movement, supporting musical awareness and concentration.

We listen to “Engine Engine Number 9” and practise keeping a steady beat using rhythm sticks. We then sing the song together and finish by using bells to play along with a video.


EYFS Areas Covered (Simple)

Communication & Language

  • Listening to the song and following simple instructions.

Physical Development

  • Using rhythm sticks and bells with simple control.

Expressive Arts & Design

  • Joining in with singing and exploring sounds on instruments.
activities.activityText:
1. Listen & Tap
  2. Children listen to Engine Engine Number 9 and tap rhythm sticks to the beat.
  3. Sing Together
  4. Children sing along with the song while continuing to tap the beat.
  5. Play Bells
  6. Children are given bells and try to play along with the video.


Engine, engine number nine,

Going down Chicago line,

If she’s polished, won’t she shine?

Engine, engine number nine,

All aboard!

Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Let’s blow the time away!



Verse 4


(Blow) It’s ten o’clock,


(Blow) It’s eleven o’clock,


(Blow) It’s twelve o’clock,


Let’s blow the time away!



Chorus x2


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Tick, tock, tick, tock.


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Let’s blow the time away!


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Tick, tock, tick, tock.


Pick a clock, pick a clock,


Let’s blow the time away!

Chinese New Year


Chinese New Year is here again,

here again, here again


Chinese New Year is here again

chunjie, chunjie, chunjie (‘sunyay’)


Look at the dragon breathing flames ...

lung, lung, lung (‘loong’)


Crackers are banging in the street …

baozhu, baozhu, baozhu (‘powd-zoo’)


Children have packets of money to spend …

qian, qian, qian (‘tee-en’)


Everyone dances through the streets …

wu, wu, wu (‘woo’)

activities.description: An action-based song with movements like waving arms, feet, and elbows. Gets kids moving while greeting.

Take a stick in this hand,

Take a stick in that.

Lay them both together,

Tap, tap, tap.


Tap them to the ceiling,

Tap them to the floor,

Tap them just in front of you,

And tap them on your knees.               (Sticks remain on knees until T continues)


Tap them to the ceiling,                        (Sung Slowly)

Tap them to the floor,                            (Sung Slowly)

Tap them just in front of you,                (Sung Quickly)

And tap them on your knees.               (Sticks remain on knees until T continues)


Tap them fast, tap them slow,

Tap them high, tap them low.

Tap them fast, tap them slow,

And place them on your knees. (Slowly)


(Repeat first verse, though slow down towards the end)

Take a stick in this hand,

Take a stick in that.

Lay them both together,

Let them tap, tap, tap. (Sticks remain on knees until T continues)
activities.activityText: Children use sticks to follow directional and tempo instructions, developing coordination, rhythm, and fine motor control. They also practice listening carefully, copying actions, and adjusting speed and movement, supporting musical awareness and concentration.

activities.activityText:
Apple Tree is a gem. . . that's the best way I can describe it. It's got a rhythm that is easy for my 1st graders to derive, is great for teaching so-mi (extracted in the first and third phrases), is great for la (extracted in the 2nd) and for do (the final phrase of the song). What's more is it has a fun game that the kids ask for over and over and over and over and over. . . . . and over again for. If you're not familiar with the song, here it is:




The game:

Formation: standing circle, holding hands, with two people (I use myself and a student volunteer) making and arch.


Action:  as the song is sung, the students process in a circular motion with the circle going under the arch formed by the teacher and student volunteer. On the word "out" the arch lowers their arms, catching whoever is under the tree at that time. The caught student then joins the arch (I refer to it as the tree). The game is repeated until all the "apples" (the students in the walking circle) are caught. As the tree grows, more students are caught. The students love this!


As I mentioned, this song is GREAT for 1st and 2nd grades. I use it for ta & ti-ti reading and so-mi-la reading in the first grade. I have created sets of tone ladders that the students use to sing the phrases. After they have derived the known solfa (the first three phrases of the song), they sing it while pointing to the solfége apples on their tone ladder.

Oh no he's gone to sleep again. I have another song that works but we have to use our hands too.



Sol - Hand like a fist – open the door. (Palm facing yourself)

Mi – I had an ok day splashed by a bus - So so


Hello, Bailey? Hello Bailey? - (Sol and Mi Hand Signals)


“He must be still sleep, Can you help me call for him?” - (Doing hand actions) Bail-ley – repeat –

“No he can’t hear us”. Change - as we learn more signals - and notes.

"I'm going to place Bailey over here so that he can listen. He's a bit shy so it might take a few session for him to join in."
activities.activityText: Children explore pitch using hand signals (Sol and Mi) while singing and calling for the puppet Bailey, developing early musical literacy. They also practice listening, following actions, and expressing emotions through gestures, supporting social, communication, and physical development.

The king is not at home

To buy his wife a comb

We listen to “Engine Engine Number 9” and practise keeping a steady beat using rhythm sticks. We then sing the song together and finish by using bells to play along with a video.


EYFS Areas Covered (Simple)

Communication & Language

  • Listening to the song and following simple instructions.

Physical Development

  • Using rhythm sticks and bells with simple control.

Expressive Arts & Design

  • Joining in with singing and exploring sounds on instruments.
activities.activityText:
1. Listen & Tap
  2. Children listen to Engine Engine Number 9 and tap rhythm sticks to the beat.
  3. Sing Together
  4. Children sing along with the song while continuing to tap the beat.
  5. Play Bells
  6. Children are given bells and try to play along with the video.


Engine, engine number nine,

Going down Chicago line,

If she’s polished, won’t she shine?

Engine, engine number nine,

All aboard!

activities.description: Children sit on the floor in a circle. The teacher rolls the ball to child, they sing/say their name as they catch the ball.

Hello Everyone

Hello Everyone

Hello Everyone

It's time for music now!


(Chords - C, G, F)


Change to jum, march on the spot (slowly, quickly), clap with me, spin around.


Hello - first child's name then continue through the group.

Finally - "Hello Bailey? - oh no. I think he's asleep".

Let's sing it again (louder, quicker, slower, quieter) and see if we can wake him up.

"He's a bit shy - he will get better as he get used to you. Let's pop him in my chair, he likes to listed".
activities.activityText: Children practice listening, taking turns, and responding to peers’ names while singing and moving to music. They develop gross motor skills through actions like marching, jumping, clapping, and spinning, while exploring voice and rhythm.

activities.description: An interactive call-and-response hello song with actions like stomping feet, dancing, and playing a beat.

Circle Chat

  • What is a robot?
  • What do you know about robots?
  • What do they look like?
  • How do they move?
  • What can/can’t they do?


Warm up


  • T models saying the lyrics in different ways/pitches.
  • Children can share their version too.


“I am a robot - I go beep-beep-beep beep-bop”

  • Child chosen to lead. Everyone has to copy their movement – they can only use their head, neck, arms and hands – stay seated.


Robot Dance


  • During ‘This is my robot dance’ section, one or two children become ‘robots’ and dance in the middle of the circle to show off their robot moves!
  • Each time the ‘I am a robot’ section returns, everyone stands still and does the robot moves on the spot, copying the leader.
  • Select new robots to dance in the middle of the circle each time the ‘This is my robot dance’ section returns.


Possible extension


  • Move like a robot activity with piano – slow, quick.
  • https://www.bbc.co.uk/cbeebies/watch/lets-go-club-robot-freeze-game

Hot Cross Buns

Hot cross buns! Hot cross buns!

One a penny, two a penny, Hot cross buns!


If you have no daughters, Give them to your sons.

One a penny, two a penny, Hot cross buns!
Moving and stopping to the beat responding to speeds, levels and actions

Nautical Commands Game - Check Canva Link


Port — Run to the left of the playing space

Starboard — Run to the right

Bow — Run to the front

Stern — Run to the back

Shark! — Put your hand on your head like a fin

Captain on Deck — Stand to attention and salute

Land Ahoy! — Shade your eyes and point to the horizon

Scrub the decks — Crouch down and pretend to clean the floor

Climb the rigging — Pretend to climb a ladder

Man the lifeboats — Sit on the floor and pretend to row

Rats! — Creep around squeaking

Seagull — Duck and cover your head

Man overboard! — Pretend to splash around or swim

custom_objectives.objective_text: Show sensitivity to their own and others' needs.
Negotiate space and obstacles safely, with consideration for themselves and others.
Demonstrate strength, balance and coordination when playing.
Move energetically, such as running, jumping, dancing, hopping, skipping and climbing.
Hold a pencil effectively in preparation for fluent writing – using the tripod grip in almost all cases.
Use a range of small tools, including scissors, paintbrushes and cutlery.
Listen attentively and respond to what they hear with relevant questions, comments and actions when being read to and during whole class discussions and small group interactions.
Begin to show accuracy and care when drawing.
Talk about the lives of the people around them and their roles in society.
Know some similarities and differences between things in the past and now, drawing on their experiences and what has been read in class.
Understand the past through settings, characters and events encountered in books read in class and storytelling.
Describe their immediate environment using knowledge from observation, discussion, stories, non-fiction texts and maps.
Know about similarities and differences between different religious and cultural communities in this country, drawing on their experiences and what has been read in class.
Explore some similarities and differences between life in this country and life in other countries, drawing on knowledge from stories, non-fiction texts and (when appropriate) maps.
Explore the natural world around them, making observations and drawing pictures of animals and plants.
Know some similarities and differences between the natural world around them and contrasting environments, drawing on their experiences and what has been read in class.
Understand some important processes and changes in the natural world around them, including the seasons and changing states of matter.
Demonstrate understanding of what has been read to them by retelling stories and narratives using their own words and recently introduced vocabulary.
Anticipate (where appropriate) key events in stories.
Use and understand recently introduced vocabulary during discussions about stories, non-fiction, rhymes and during role-play.
Say a sound for each letter in the alphabet and at least 10 digraphs.
Read words consistently with their phonic knowledge by sound-blending.
Read aloud simple sentences and books that are consistent with their phonic knowledge, including some common exception words.
Write recognisable letters, most of which are correctly formed.
Spell words by identifying sounds in them and representing the sounds with a letter or letters.
Write simple phrases and sentences that can be read by others.
Have a deep understanding of number to 10, including the composition of each number.
Subitise (recognise quantities without counting) up to 5.

Automatically recall (without reference to rhymes, counting or other aids) number bonds up to 5 (including subtraction facts) and some number bonds to 10, including double facts and how quantities can be distributed equally.
Verbally count beyond 20, recognising the pattern of the counting system.
Compare quantities up to 10 in different contexts, recognising when one quantity is greater than, less than or the same as the other quantity.
Explore and represent patterns within numbers up to 10, including evens and odds, double facts and how quantities can be distributed equally.
Safely use and explore a variety of materials, tools and techniques, experimenting with colour, design, texture, form and function.
Share their creations, explaining the processes they have used.
Make use of props and materials when role-playing characters in narratives and stories.
Invent, adapt and recount narratives and stories with peers and their teacher.
Sing a range of well-known nursery rhymes and songs.
Perform songs, rhymes, poems and stories with others, and (when appropriate) try to move in time with music.
Participate in small group, class and one-to-one discussions, offering their own ideas, using recently introduced vocabulary.
Offer explanations for why things might happen, making use of recently introduced vocabulary from stories, non-fiction, rhymes and poems when appropriate.

Express their ideas and feelings about their experiences using full sentences, including use of past, present and future tenses and making use of conjunctions, with modelling and support from their teacher.
Show an understanding of their own feelings and those of others, and begin to regulate their behaviour accordingly.
Make comments about what they have heard and ask questions to clarify their understanding.
Set and work towards simple goals, being able to wait for what they want and control their immediate impulses when appropriate.
Give focused attention to what the teacher says, responding appropriately even when engaged in activity, and show an ability to follow instructions involving several ideas or actions.
Be confident to try new activities and show independence, resilience and perseverance in the face of challenge.
Explain the reasons for rules, know right from wrong and try to behave accordingly.
Manage their own basic hygiene and personal needs, including dressing, going to the toilet and understanding the importance of healthy food choices.
Work and play cooperatively and take turns with others.
Form positive attachments to adults and friendships with peers.
Hold conversation when engaged in back-and-forth exchanges with their teacher and peers.
Experiment with different voices and ways of speaking to represent different characters.
Listen to and respond appropriately to other characters when speaking in role.
Use recently introduced vocabulary when speaking in role and during dramatic activities.
Express ideas and feelings through dramatic expression and spoken language.
Respond to questions about drama and characters using sentences and descriptive language.
Begin to develop understanding of how language and tone can convey character and emotion.
Role: Understand that in drama, they can take on the role or character of someone else.
Imagination: Use imagination to create and develop ideas in dramatic play and role-play.
Story: Begin to understand that drama can tell stories and communicate ideas to others.
Audience: Develop awareness that drama is performed or shared with others to watch and listen.
Character: Explore how characters behave, feel and interact with others in dramas and stories.
Adopt a range of character roles from familiar stories, rhymes and play scenarios with teacher support.
Sustain a simple character role for short periods of time during dramatic play and role-play activities.
Respond appropriately when others interact with them in role, maintaining the character's behaviour.
Use facial expressions, gestures and simple actions to communicate their character to others.
Begin to explore how characters might feel, think and behave in different situations through dramatic play.
Express ideas about what a character might do or say next in a story or scenario.
Respond to questions about characters' motivations and feelings in a drama activity.
Identify similarities and differences between different characters from stories and dramas.
Participate in guided improvisation activities, responding to simple scenarios and prompts provided by the teacher.
Contribute ideas for how a simple dramatic scene might develop or progress.
Use their imagination to develop simple dialogue and actions within familiar story contexts.
Experiment with different ways of moving, speaking and responding in role-play situations.
Work with peers to create short, simple dramatic scenes from familiar stories or experiences.
Use props, objects and simple staging to help establish and develop their drama.
Respond creatively to teacher-led "what if" questions and scenarios.
Begin to understand that drama communicates ideas and stories to others.
Follow and join in with simple scripts and repeated phrases from stories and rhymes.
Recite familiar rhymes, poems and repeated refrains with appropriate expression and rhythm.
Speak lines or phrases clearly when performing or taking turns in dramatic activities.
Begin to understand the concept of a script and how actors learn lines.
Participate in whole-class and small group performances of familiar stories and rhymes.
Speak audibly and clearly when presenting drama or performing with others.
Show awareness of an audience when performing, maintaining focus and engagement.
Demonstrate increasing confidence when sharing their dramatic work with peers and adults.
Participate in repeated run-throughs of simple dramatic activities to improve their performance.
Respond to teacher guidance about how to improve their dramatic work and performance.
Make simple choices about how to perform a role or action based on feedback.
Show awareness of when they have performed well and when they might improve.
Work cooperatively with peers during rehearsal and preparation of dramatic activities.
Listen to others' ideas and suggestions about how to develop and improve their drama.
Take turns and respect others' contributions during group dramatic work.
Begin to understand the importance of practice and teamwork in drama.
Share their dramatic work with peers, the class or adults in the school.
Perform simple dramatic scenes or activities they have created or rehearsed.

Character: Explore and develop complex characters with distinct personalities, motivations and emotions.
Plot: Understand how stories are structured and begin to create logical sequences of dramatic action.
Audience: Develop awareness of how to engage and communicate with audiences through performance.

Emotion: Explore and express a range of emotions through role, voice and movement.
Tension and Conflict: Begin to understand how drama creates and resolves conflict and tension.
